root.title: Cúram  8.1.2
root.summary: Cúram 8.1.2 Server Developer’s Guide. This node is the main entry point for server-side development and runtime reference material for the model-driven Cúram platform. It covers application/component directory structure and component precedence via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, including localized component overrides. It also explains build files and targets, configuration settings, Data Manager, SQL Checker, Eclipse/SDEJ setup, logging (log4j 2), exceptions, message and code table files, deprecation, and user preferences. Runtime topics include transaction control, transaction SQL cache, deferred processing, timers, events and handlers, unique IDs, and application resource localization/fallback. Configuration chapters cover Bootstrap.properties, dynamic/static/variable properties in Application.prx, and infrastructure auditing defaults. The section is useful if you need build, configuration, localization, server API, or runtime-behavior guidance. Front and back matter include a note directing readers to Notices on page 215, legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This section is a brief front-matter note directing readers to review the Notices section on page 215 before using the product or information. It also includes the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Cúram 8.1.2 edition/front-matter pages covering scope and copyright, then the start of “Cúram server developer.” Introduces model-driven server development and the main topics in building/configuring a Cúram application. Covers server directory and component structure, component order and localized components, source vs built application layout, and key build outputs/directories (datamanager, ear, jar, sqlcheck, svr, cls, gen, ddl). Useful if you need project structure, build artifacts, or navigation to setup and tooling sections.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Cúram SDEJ installation and build guidance: directory structure of the installed environment, Ant build entry points, required environment variables, optional build/configuration properties, Java compiler and runtime settings, generator options, Linux encoding workaround, and the internal build flow. It also explains generated artifacts from model processing, including wsconnector, emx2xml, modelgen, and the resulting XML/data definition files and Java code categories. Useful for understanding how to configure, run, and troubleshoot server builds.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section catalogs Cúram server build targets and generated artifacts. It covers deployment and web service outputs, message/code table/event generation (msggen, ctgen, evgen), compilation steps, utility targets for database, passwords, tests, javadoc, cleaning, release packaging, supplement builds, and API restriction reporting. It also explains Clover coverage targets and extensive ruleset management targets for listing, export/import, validation, and rules function merging. Finally, it introduces IEG validation and application configuration import/export and merge precedence rules.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Covers Cúram build/deployment and configuration targets. Describes tab/application configuration import/export, workflow validation/import targets and prerequisites, deployment targets for application servers, and how to extend or override Ant build scripts. Also introduces CREOLE and evidence generation targets, then explains application properties in Application.prx, including dynamic vs static properties, file merging by SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, and rules for overriding, adding, or removing properties and locales.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: Cúram configuration and database setup topics. Explains Bootstrap.properties purpose, property precedence/caching (Java system > Application.prx > Bootstrap.properties), and EAR-generated Bootstrap settings. Covers multiple time zone support and how dates vs date/times are stored/displayed. Introduces Data Manager for initial database creation, MBCS expansion planning, invocation, and warns it is not for upgrades. Includes database artifact formats: XML table/constraint metadata and DMX data files, with element/attribute rules for tables, columns, rows, and attributes.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Explains DMX file structure and customization in Cúram Data Manager. Covers <value> attributes (language, country), locale rules, non-localized primary keys, and valid value types by column type (number, text, bool, id, blob, clob, date, timestamp). Describes how to customize DMX files by adding files under SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/data/{initial,demo,test}, merged by SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER. Details merge rules for override, row insertion/removal, and locale-specific value copying, with examples showing table override, row remove, locale merging, and row-level locales.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: Covers Cúram Data Manager and database tooling: DMX merge/override rules, locale selection and fallback, DMX validation and tracing, primary key/index/constraint naming, automatic index generation, z/OS tablespace behavior, datamanager_config.xml targets, database synchronization/export, statistics collection, LOB loading for BLOB/CLOB files, and SQL Checker basics/limitations. Useful for users working on database build, data loading, localization, or SQL validation.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-84
children.summary: Cúram SDEJ development guidance covering Eclipse setup, project structure, and logging/tracing. It explains importing the four Eclipse projects, project config files (.project, .classpath, .settings), generated classpaths, access rules, and working sets. It also covers Trace/log4j2 usage: logging categories, trace levels and properties, and log4j2 configuration for file or socket output. Includes notes on SQLJ checker limitations and platform portability warnings, plus behavior of user/alternate user fields under different security and deferred-process settings.

children.title: Edition — pages 85-94
children.summary: Covers Cúram server developer guidance on performance statistics logging, localized log messages, dynamic UIM tracing, and exception handling. Explains checked vs unchecked exceptions, RIL behavior, exception construction and chaining, logging exceptions, general coding guidelines, common DAL/database exceptions, the Record Not Found indicator to avoid exceptions, localized output via LocalisableString, and the InformationalManager for grouping multiple validation messages.

children.title: Edition — pages 95-104
children.summary: Cúram server developer reference covering InformationalManager behavior and logging, then message file format and customization. Explains msggen build output, message.xml elements (<messages>, <message>, <locale>), merge precedence via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, locale fallback rules, and server log localization. Also covers localizing SDEJ .iml message files. The section ends with code table file fundamentals, ctgen outputs, and the codetables/codetable/codetabledata XML structure and attributes.

children.title: Edition — pages 105-114
children.summary: Defines code table XML structure and customization rules. Covers attributes and subelements for codetabledata, locale, displaynames, name, code, views, view, description, annotation, and comments, including locale support via SERVER_LOCALE_LIST. Explains how code table files are overridden and merged across components using SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, with rules for merging codes, locales, display names, views, and overwrite/default_code behavior. Includes sample merge outcomes. Also describes ctgen outputs: generated Java symbol definitions and SQL inserts for CodeTableHeader, CodeTableItem, CodeTableView, and CodeTableViewCode.

children.title: Edition — pages 115-124
children.summary: Covers Cúram code table generation and localized artifacts: Java symbol files, SQL inserts, properties files for display names, hierarchy definitions, locale fallback, and localization of shipped SDEJ .itx code tables. Also explains code table precedence, package rules, unsupported views in hierarchies, and generated SQL locations. The latter pages introduce specialized readmulti operations: how they differ from standard readmulti, when to use them, implementation steps, helper classes, and cautions about Post Data Access/On-fail memory use and maximum-record limits.

children.title: Edition — pages 125-134
children.summary: Introduces Cúram deprecation: why artifacts are deprecated, how they remain supported temporarily, and how deprecation is marked in docs, code, and build warnings. Explains which artifact types can be deprecated, notes entities are handled specially, and lists limitations of deprecation tagging/warnings. Describes how to run the deprecation report build, configure reporting, and interpret summary logs, including distinguishing simple references from customizations via [deprecation] and [customization] tags. Also notes IEG scripts require manual checking for deprecated dependencies.

children.title: Edition — pages 135-144
children.summary: Covers deprecation handling and log analysis, then user preferences: defining/overriding DefaultPreferences.xml, required XML fields, locale-specific properties files, merge precedence, runtime access, and external-user visibility/localization. Also introduces transaction runtime behavior: Facade methods typically map to one bean-managed transaction, locking/optimistic locking via forUpdate, DB2/Oracle isolation behavior, and performance guidelines. Ends with the transaction SQL query cache: transaction-scoped caching of SELECT results, what is cached, exceptions, and invalidation on updates/commit/rollback.

children.title: Edition — pages 145-154
children.summary: Covers Cúram server developer topics from SQL query caching through deferred processing and timers. Explains transaction SQL query cache behavior, invalidation triggers, configuration, runtime APIs, logging, and LOB size limits. Describes deferred process modeling with `<<wmdpactivity>>`, `WMInstanceData`, `DeferredProcessing.startProcess()`, `DPProcess` configuration, error handling via `DPCallback`, and security context. Also introduces Cúram timer beans, `TimerInfo`, `TimerTask`, `TimerCallback`, supported timer types, and rules/limitations for timer-driven operations.

children.title: Edition — pages 155-164
children.summary: Covers Cúram timers and events, plus Unique IDs. Timer rules include persistence in app servers, no catch-up after downtime, exceptions roll back but do not cancel repeating timers, and timers should be short-running and not batch-driven. Development uses java.util.Timer with non-transactional, non-persistent limits. Events are defined in .evx XML, merged by component precedence, generate Java constants and DMX scripts, and are raised synchronously via EventService. Also explains event handlers/filters, registration, and Unique ID key sets for human-readable or random database keys.

children.title: Edition — pages 165-174
children.summary: Cúram Unique IDs: purpose, when to use them, when not to, human-readable vs non-human-readable behavior, gaps/holes, key sets, KeyServer table fields, and how to allocate IDs via `UniqueID.nextUniqueID()`. Covers the Range Aware Key Server for CTM, including key block ranges, group/range rules, and usage limits. Also begins configuration parameters: `Bootstrap.properties` database/environment/test/custom settings and their key properties, including DB type, Oracle cache/connection options, environment formats, test overrides, and custom workflow BPO.

children.title: Edition — pages 175-184
children.summary: Lists Cúram server configuration properties across several areas: environment settings, JMX monitoring/tracing/statistics, test-only options, rules infrastructure, IEG behavior, custom extension hooks, trace/logging controls, security/authentication, SMTP mail client settings, and the start of XML Server settings. Includes defaults and notes on disabled/unsupported use cases, especially for deployed apps and CER rules. Useful as a reference node for locating specific server property names and meanings.

children.title: Edition — pages 185-194
children.summary: Cúram server developer configuration reference covering Application.prx/static properties and infrastructure auditing. Includes XML Server, database, KeyServer, BatchLauncher, Workflow, CTM, Custom, Security, Trace, and Environment settings, with property names, types, defaults, and meanings. Also explains KeyServer keyset caching for batch performance, transaction timeout properties, audit-related overrides, and infrastructure default table-level auditing settings for database operations. Useful for locating specific runtime/configuration flags, custom hooks, security/authentication controls, and audit behavior.

children.title: Edition — pages 195-204
children.summary: This section is a long audit-settings table from Cúram Server Developer 8.1.2 (pages 195–204). It lists many server operations and their default auditing values, mostly False or N/A, across areas such as ActivityInstance, Batch*, CodeTable*, Event*, Group*, IEG*, JMSLiteMessage, ProcessDefinition, and related workflow/data objects. Useful if you need the default audit configuration for specific operations or to verify which methods are audited by default.

children.title: Edition — pages 205-214
children.summary: This section is a Cúram server developer reference table listing default auditing settings for many operations across workflow, security, task, user preference, template, history, and XML/archive entities. It shows which operations default to auditing on, off, or N/A, including ProcessDefinition/Instance, Properties/PropDescription, RuleSet, SecurityGroup/Role/Identifier, Task and TaskHistory, WorkflowDeadline/History, Users, WDO/XSL/XMLTemplate, and related insert/read/modify/remove/search methods. Useful for checking audit defaults for specific server APIs.

Cúram 8.1.2 server developer reference. Covers model-driven server development, application/component directory structure, component order and localized components, build targets, configuration settings, Data Manager and SQL Checker, Eclipse setup, logging, exceptions, message/code table files, deprecation, user preferences, transaction control, deferred processing, timers, events, unique IDs, Bootstrap/Application.prx properties, and infrastructure auditing defaults. Useful as the main navigation node for build, runtime, configuration, localization, and server API behavior.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram 8.1.2 developer guide. Lists major chapters and page numbers for server development, including building/configuring applications, SDEJ APIs, application resources, runtime behavior, configuration parameters, and infrastructure auditing settings, plus front/back matter such as notes, edition, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful as a navigation index rather than substantive technical content.

children.title: 1 Cúram server developer
1.1 Building and configuring a Cúram application
Directory Structure
Application components
children.summary: Explains how Cúram server application artifacts are organized into components under `<EJBServer>/components`. Covers component folders, the core component, and how `SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER` sets priority for same-named artifacts using a comma-separated list with highest priority first; core is always lowest and omitted. Notes that unlisted components are appended alphabetically, or all components default to alphabetical order. Also describes localized components (`<component>_<locale>`), which are automatically prepended based on `SERVER_LOCALE_LIST`, and introduces application directory structure basics.

children.title: Application directory structure
Source artifacts of the Cúram application
children.summary: Describes the source-artifact directory structure of a Cúram application before build, using placeholders SERVER_DIR, ProjectPackage, and CodePackage. Explains the roles of top-level directories like project and components, and the pre-defined components/core tree. Lists key artifact locations such as codetable, events, message, model, properties, rulesets, lib, and their generated outputs or uses, plus build and project metadata files. Useful for understanding where source files, configuration, and generated artifacts reside.

children.title: Cúram application build structure
children.summary: Explains the Cúram server application build layout and what each generated directory contains. Covers source/build-related folders under components (sample, webservices, workflow, wsdl, custom source) and key build files (build.bat/.sh, build.xml, deprecationreport.xml, buildhelp.bat). Describes the post-build SERVER_DIR structure, including build/datamanager, build/ear for WAS/WLS, build/jar, build/sqlcheck, and build/svr with compiled classes, generated sources, ddl, webservices, workflow, and build logs.

Explains the Cúram server application directory structure before and after build. Covers component organization and priority via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, localized components, and the source-artifact layout under SERVER_DIR (project, core component subfolders like codetable, events, message, model, properties, rulesets, lib, sample, webservices, workflow, wsdl, plus custom source). Also describes build outputs under build/ (datamanager, ear/WAS/WLS, jar, sqlcheck, svr with compiled classes, generated sources, DDL, webservices, workflow, and logs).

children.title: Artifacts of the SDEJ
children.summary: Describes the Cúram SDEJ installation structure and generated build artifacts. It lists key build output directories under build/svr (codetable, events, message, webservices, workflow, wsc2, app.xml, buildlogs) and explains the top-level SDEJ folders under CURAMSDEJ: bin, codetable, doc, drivers, ear, lib, message, rsa, scripts, util, and xmlserver. Useful for understanding what each directory contains and where build, configuration, localization, database, and XML server resources are located.

Covers Cúram server directory structure: component folders under `<EJBServer>/components`, component priority via `SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER`, and localized components prepended from `SERVER_LOCALE_LIST`. Describes source-artifact layout in `SERVER_DIR` (project, core and custom source areas) and build outputs under `build/` (datamanager, EARs, jars, SQL check, compiled/generated server artifacts, logs). Also outlines SDEJ installation structure under `CURAMSDEJ` (bin, codetable, drivers, ear, lib, message, rsa, scripts, util, xmlserver).

children.title: Build files and their targets
How to initiate the build
children.summary: This section explains how to start a Cúram application build from the SDEJ using Ant. It covers running buildhelp to view targets, then initiating the main build with build server from SERVER_DIR after setting required environment variables such as JAVA_HOME, J2EE_JAR, ANT_HOME, SERVER_DIR, and SERVER_MODEL_NAME. It also notes how to override the default junit.jar location with the JUNIT_JAR system property and introduces optional build settings passed as -D parameters, including dir.sde.

children.title: Overriding default JUNIT.JAR
children.summary: Explains how the Cúram SDEJ build uses Ant and where build files and targets are located, including how to start a build from SERVER_DIR with required environment variables. The section also documents overriding the default junit.jar location: by default it is taken from JUNIT_HOME, but the JUNIT_JAR system property can replace it and takes precedence. It begins the build configuration options for `build server -D...`, including the `dir.sde` parameter and its default.

children.title: How to configure the build
Cúram Build Settings
children.summary: Explains how the Cúram Server Development Environment uses Ant build files and how to start a build from SERVER_DIR with build server/buildhelp. Covers required setup variables (JAVA_HOME, J2EE_JAR, ANT_HOME, SERVER_DIR, SERVER_MODEL_NAME), overriding JUNIT_JAR, and build-time -D settings. Key options include SDEJ location, log level, property-file directory, code table length validation, warning/error handling, forcing model generation, non-interned strings, DBCS/non-ASCII model handling, extra generator options, portability checks, facade generation, schema validation, and app server fail-on-error.

children.title: Java Compiler Settings
children.summary: This section lists Java compiler build parameters and their effects. It explains how to pass them on the build command (for example, `build server -Dcmp.debug=on`) and provides defaults for settings such as debug info, memory limit, warnings, optimization, deprecation info, verbose output, runtime classpath inclusion, and whether compilation errors fail the build. Useful for configuring or troubleshooting the Java build process.

children.title: Java Task Settings
children.summary: This section lists build-time Java task settings for Cúram server development. It explains how to pass parameters via `-Djava.fork=true` and documents `java.fork` (run external classes in another VM, default true), `java.maxmemory` (forked VM memory, default 768m), `java.failonerror` (stop on nonzero exit, default true), and `java.jvmargs` (arguments for the forked VM, default empty). It also shows nearby build options like `cmp.listfiles`, `cmp.gc`, `PRE_CLASSPATH`, and `POST_CLASSPATH`.

children.title: Generator Settings
children.summary: This section lists build and runtime configuration parameters for the Cúram server generator and related tasks. It covers compiler options like listing source files and garbage collection mode, classpath variables for adding JARs before or after the classpath, Java task settings such as forked VM usage, memory, error handling, and JVM arguments, and introduces generator-specific options passed via `-Dextra.generator.options`. Useful for finding build behavior controls and parameter defaults.

children.title: LANG Environment variable for Linux®
children.summary: Explains a Linux build workaround for Red Hat Enterprise Linux when compilation fails with “unmappable character for encoding UTF8”; set `LANG=en_US.ISO-8859-1` to address the Windows/Linux encoding mismatch. Also begins a “What is happening under the hood” explanation of build targets, noting the `generated` target generates and compiles code for IDE use and includes web service connector generation from registered WSDL files.

Explains how Cúram Server Development Environment builds are run from SERVER_DIR using Ant (`build server`, `buildhelp`) and what environment variables must be set (`JAVA_HOME`, `J2EE_JAR`, `ANT_HOME`, `SERVER_DIR`, `SERVER_MODEL_NAME`). Lists build-time `-D` options for build settings, Java compiler settings, and Java task/runtime settings, including logging, property-file location, code table validation limits, warning/error behavior, model generation, DBCS/non-ASCII handling, portability checks, facade generation, schema validation, and JVM/classpath controls.

children.title: What is happening under the hood
generated
wsconnector
children.summary: Describes the wsconnector build step, which generates outbound web service client stubs from .wsdl files listed in <SERVER_DIR>/project/config/webservices_config.xml. Explains the XML service format, that the WSDL path is relative to SERVER_DIR, connectors can be enabled/disabled by configuration, and the file may be empty. Notes generated stubs are not source and are overwritten on each build to stay synchronized with the WSDLs.

children.title: emx2xml
children.summary: Describes the emx2xml build step in Cúram server development. emx2xml transforms the UML model in <SERVER_DIR>/components/*/model into an intermediate XML representation stored at the top level of the directory tree. The section sits among other build steps (modelgen, msggen, ctgen, evgen, compile.generated) and immediately precedes details on wsconnector and modelgen outputs.

children.title: modelgen
children.summary: Describes the modelgen build target, which converts the intermediate XML model into generated artifacts and removes obsolete ones. It produces data definition XML files (tables, keys, constraints, batch, security/fields-returned, SQLJ) that are merged into build/ddl, plus Java code under build/svr/gen with categories like intf, fact, base, and remote. It also generates deployment support and web service support artifacts, and notes that generated web service connectors are overwritten each build.

children.title: msggen
children.summary: This section explains Cúram message files and the msggen build target. Message files in a component’s /message directory support localization and can override shipped platform messages via SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>, with precedence controlled by SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER. msggen merges same-named message files, stores the merged result in /build/svr/message/scp, generates Java source and property files for runtime access, compiles the code, and packages it into /build/jar/messages.jar.

children.title: ctgen
children.summary: This section introduces generated artifacts in Cúram build outputs, including deployment metadata for IBM WebSphere and Oracle WebLogic, web service artifacts under /build/svr/gen, and message generation via msggen for localized message files, merging component overrides into Java/property outputs. It then begins ctgen, explaining that Cúram code table files provide indirection for commonly used constants stored in the database to improve storage efficiency.

children.title: evgen
children.summary: Describes Cúram build processing for events: event files in a component’s events directory are merged by the evgen target, translated into generated Java constants and .dmx database-population files, then compiled into /build/jar/events.jar. Explains that events enable loosely coupled communication via listeners. This follows a similar pattern to codetable generation and sits near the compile.generated and implemented build targets.

children.title: compile.generated
children.summary: `compile.generated` compiles generated source that does not depend on the `impl` directory. It covers generated classes under `build/svr/gen` matching `**/struct/**/*.java`, `**/intf/**/*.java`, and `<Project Package>/*.java`. The step uses an augmented Ant dependency checker to reduce build time.

Covers the generated build pipeline in Cúram: wsconnector/wsconnector2 create outbound web service client stubs from WSDLs listed in `webservices_config.xml` (relative to `SERVER_DIR`, configurable on/off, regenerated each build); emx2xml extracts UML into intermediate XML; modelgen turns that XML into DDL, Java, deployment, and web service artifacts and removes obsolete outputs; msggen merges localized message files into Java/property resources and `messages.jar`; ctgen begins code table generation for database-indirection constants.

children.title: implemented
compile.implemented
children.summary: Describes the `compile.implemented` Ant step, which compiles all hand-crafted source and any generated code not already built by `compile.generated`. It lists the generated and source path patterns included from `build/svr/gen` and `components/*/source`. Also introduces utility Ant targets, especially database-related ones: `database`, `prepare.application.data`, `extractdata`, `reloadextracteddata`, and `checksql`, with notes on when they must be run and what they validate or transform.

This section defines the `implemented` Ant target, which finishes the build by wrapping `compile.implemented`. That target compiles all hand-crafted source plus any generated code not already built by `compile.generated`, using an augmented Ant dependency checker to reduce build time. The surrounding page also describes codetable generation (`ctgen`), the `prp.noninternedstrings` setting and its dependency-checking caveat, and event generation (`evgen`) from `.evx` files into Java constants and database `.dmx` files.

Explains Cúram’s build pipeline “under the hood” via Ant targets. Covers generated steps: wsconnector/wsconnector2 create outbound web service stubs from WSDLs in `webservices_config.xml`; emx2xml extracts UML to intermediate XML; modelgen generates DDL, Java, deployment, and web service artifacts and removes obsolete outputs; msggen merges localized messages into Java/property files and `messages.jar`; ctgen generates code table artifacts and warns about `prp.noninternedstrings` dependency-check issues; evgen builds event constants and `.dmx` files. Also describes `compile.generated` and `implemented` build compilation.

children.title: Utility Targets
children.summary: Lists Cúram server utility Ant targets beyond normal builds. Covers database tasks (database, prepare.application.data, extract/reload data, checksql, foreignkeycheck), password tools (encrypt, digest), test and environment validation (test, configtest, configreport), properties and Javadoc generation, and other maintenance targets (clean, mergeshortnames, deprecationreport, release, model, runbatch, runstatistics). Includes key usage notes, dependencies, and warnings such as rerunning prepare.application.data after database changes and running database before checksql.

children.title: Clover Targets
children.summary: This section lists Ant targets for Clover integration and ruleset XML management in Cúram. Clover targets require clover.jar and clover.license in <ANT_HOME>/lib and include clover.server, clover.supplement, clover.report.html, and clover.report.viewer for instrumented builds and coverage reporting. It also introduces rules targets for listing and exporting rulesets from the database to XML for source control or machine transfer, including listrulesets and exportruleset with rulesetid and component parameters.

children.title: Rules Targets
children.summary: Describes Ant targets for rule set management in Cúram. Covers listing rulesets, exporting a ruleset XML from database to a component rulesets folder, importing a ruleset XML into the database with optional overwrite, and validating rulesets either across the whole application or in a specified directory. Notes that rulesets are the system of record in the database, XML export/import is for source control or transfer only, and validation checks schema and unique rule set IDs.

children.title: IEG Targets
children.summary: This section briefly introduces the IEG validation target. It states that `validateieg2scripts` checks intelligent evidence gathering (IEG) scripts in a specified directory. The surrounding text then begins the next topic, “Application Configuration Import and Export Targets,” describing how client application XML and properties files are merged and loaded into the database, with precedence rules for `clientapps` versus `tab` directories.

children.title: Application Configuration Import and Export Targets
children.summary: Describes application configuration import/export for the Cúram web client. Config is stored as XML and properties files in the server source tree, merged at build time, loaded into the database, and read by the client at runtime. Key merge rules: clientapps files override tab files regardless of component order; clientapps files are selected, not merged, based on component order. This section helps when looking for how client-side configuration files are combined and prioritized.

children.title: Workflow Targets
children.summary: Describes workflow-related Ant targets for Cúram. It explains that workflow process definitions are stored in the database but can also be validated, imported, and listed from XML files. Validation targets include validateworkflows, validateallworkflows, and validateworkflow, each supporting optional schema-only validation; referenced rule sets and events must already be loaded. Import targets are importworkflow and importworkflows with optional overwrite. Also defines required properties workflow.dir and workflow.file.

children.title: Deployment Targets
children.summary: Lists the deployment targets available for Cúram applications. Covers server-wide configuration targets, EAR build targets for WebSphere, WebLogic, and Liberty (including Web Services variants), JSP precompilation, install/uninstall of EAR applications, required application data preparation after database setup, and basic server control commands. Also notes that the server module EAR must be deployed before client-only EARs, and that prepare.application.data must be rerun after rerunning the database target.

children.title: Extending the Build
Introducing a new script
children.summary: Explains how to introduce a new top-level Ant build script by setting CDEJ_BUILDFILE or SDEJ_BUILDFILE to point to a custom build.xml that imports its parent (e.g., EJBServer/build.xml). Describes using Ant import to extend, enhance, or override existing OOTB targets, with the main file’s targets taking precedence. Includes examples for adding new server scripts and enhancing existing targets via antcall to the inherited target name (e.g., app_database.database).

Explains how to extend the Ant build by creating a top-level custom build.xml that imports the delivered parent script (for example, EJBServer/build.xml). A custom script is selected via CDEJ_BUILDFILE or SDEJ_BUILDFILE, then can add new targets or enhance/override existing ones. Because imported targets defer to the main file, the custom script takes precedence. Also shows how to call the inherited target using antcall with the original project-qualified name, such as app_database.database.

children.title: Overridden Targets
children.summary: Explains “Overridden Targets” in the SDEJ build system: some application build script targets replace SDEJ targets and only the unqualified target name should be used (for example, websphereEAR, not serverbuild.websphereEAR). Lists affected targets such as app_auxiliary.ctgen/msggen, app_runtimewas.configure, and several serverbuild.* targets. Notes that only documented targets visible via -projecthelp should be enhanced or invoked. Also briefly introduces application targets, including biapp.configure.birtviewer for WebSphere BIRT Viewer deployment, excluding Kubernetes.

children.title: Application Targets
BI App
children.summary: Describes the BI App target biapp.configure.birtviewer, which configures the BIRT Viewer application for IBM WebSphere Application Server after the BIRT EAR is installed. It accepts Ant properties for server.name and application.name. Notes that this target does not apply to Kubernetes deployments, where BIRT is managed by a Helm chart.

children.title: CREOLE
children.summary: Lists CREOLE build/validation targets and evidence generation targets. CREOLE entries cover checking uploaded DMX rule-set XML, compiling/generating test classes, consolidating rules and resources, creating catalogs/schema/rule docs, reporting coverage/unused attributes, uploading and validating rule sets. The evidence generation section lists EG Tool tasks for client/server generation and cleanup, wizard DMX generation, post-modelgen steps, and adding root nodes to AppResource/InitialAppResource DMX files.

children.title: Evidence Generation
children.summary: Lists build targets for evidence generation. Main items include egtools.generate for generating all evidence components, server/client generate and clean targets, wizard DMX generation, resource ID assignment, and post-modelgen processing. Also notes helper targets that append root nodes to APPRESOURCE.dmx and INITIALAPPRESOURCE.dmx, plus a clean target to remove generated components. Useful if you need the available Ant/build tasks for Cúram evidence generation.

Lists OOTB application Ant targets available via `-projecthelp`. Covers `biapp.configure.birtviewer` for configuring the BIRT Viewer on WebSphere after EAR install, using `server.name` and `application.name`; it is not used for Kubernetes, where Helm manages BIRT. Also enumerates CREOLE targets for rule-set validation, compilation, consolidation, catalog/schema/doc generation, coverage/unused-attribute reporting, upload, and rule-set validation. Includes evidence generation targets for generating, cleaning, and post-processing server/client/wizard DMX and resource files.

Explains how the SDEJ uses Ant build files in /bin, how to start builds with buildhelp and build server, required env vars, and overriding JUNIT_JAR. Documents many build options (-D settings for logging, code table checks, DBCS/non-ASCII, portability, facades, schema validation, compiler and Java runtime). Also covers internal build targets (modelgen, msggen, ctgen, evgen), utility, Clover, rules, IEG, workflow, deployment, extension/override mechanics, and application targets such as BIRT, CREOLE, and evidence generation.

children.title: Cúram Configuration Settings
Application Properties
Application prx
How to merge an application prx file
children.summary: Explains how Application.prx files are merged across components using SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence (leftmost highest, core lowest) and notes that changing the order requires rerunning build insertproperties. Describes what can be customized in PRX merges: add properties, override descriptions/display names/value/default, add locales, or mark properties removed; cannot change name, type, category, or static/dynamic setting. Includes example merge behavior showing duplicate properties overridden by higher-precedence components and locale entries combined.

Explains Application.prx properties used at runtime and loaded to the database, including dynamic vs. static behavior. Describes PRX structure (property, name, type, value, default, category, locales). Covers merging Application.prx files across components using SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence, with core lowest and unspecified components ordered alphabetically; changing precedence requires rerunning build insertproperties. Merge rules allow adding properties/locales, overriding value/default/display text/description, or marking removed, but not changing name, type, category, or dynamic/static setting.

children.title: Bootstrap.properties
children.summary: Explains the Bootstrap.properties file, which holds the minimum database connection settings and is generally only read directly from this file, not Application.prx. Notes that dynamic properties here can be overridden in the database via the administration interface. Describes property caching and priority: Java System properties override Application.prx, which override Bootstrap.properties, with a publish required to rebuild the cache. Includes example entries such as tnameserv port, bindings location, DB2 credentials and server details, Oracle service name, and H2 mode.

Cúram application properties covers runtime configuration via Application.prx and Bootstrap.properties. It explains PRX structure (name, dynamic flag, type, value, default, category, locales) and how Application.prx is loaded to the database, cached at runtime, and updated via publish/admin for dynamic properties. It details component-based PRX merging using SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence and what can/can’t be overridden. Bootstrap.properties is the minimal DB connection file; Java System properties override Application.prx, which override Bootstrap.properties. Includes sample DB2, Oracle, and H2 settings.

children.title: Support for multiple time zones
children.summary: Explains how to enable multiple time zone support in Cúram. Each user must have a time zone ID set in user preferences, using a value returned by `java.util.TimeZone.getAvailableIDs()`. Only Date/Time fields are converted to the user’s preferred time zone; Date-only and Time-only fields are not. The server stores Date/Times as epoch milliseconds, and the web tier converts between server time and user time zone. Includes the distinction between when to use Date vs Date/Time based on time zone relevance.

children.title: Dates and date/times in Cúram
children.summary: Explains how Cúram handles dates and date/times, focusing on time zone behavior between server, database timestamps, and client display. Includes an example where a record is stamped in server time (GMT) but shown to a user in a different time zone as an adjusted local time. Also references supported server time zone configurations and points to the Deployment Guide for detailed setup.

Covers Cúram environment and date/time configuration. Explains Application.prx properties: structure, dynamic vs static behavior, loading/caching, runtime updates via publish/admin, and component-based merging with SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence and merge rules. Describes Bootstrap.properties as the minimal DB connection file and the runtime property priority: Java System properties > Application.prx > Bootstrap.properties. Also explains enabling multiple time zone support, user time zone IDs, and how Cúram stores/converts Date/Time values versus Date-only/Time-only fields, with examples of server/client time behavior.

children.title: Data Manager
Intended Data Manager process
Planning for MBCS data
children.summary: Explains planning for MBCS data in Cúram: DB2/DB2 for z/OS need special configuration so Data Manager handles multibyte data correctly, and this is enabled by default. It describes column expansion for byte-based DB sizes, controlled by Bootstrap.properties properties curam.db.multibyte.expansion and curam.db.multibyte.default.factor (1.0–4.0). It warns that maximum expansion avoids errors but increases disk, memory, CPU, and buffer pool costs, and notes expansion can be reduced or disabled for single-byte-only environments, though that is not recommended.

Explains the intended Data Manager workflow for initial database creation: create the database/tablespaces, use Data Manager to create tables and load initial data, then let a DBA finish tuning and constraints. It is not for upgrading existing databases. Also covers MBCS planning: Cúram enables multibyte support by default for DB2/DB2 for z/OS by expanding string columns, controlled by Bootstrap.properties. The expansion factor ranges from 1.0 to 4.0; maximum expansion avoids errors but costs disk, memory, CPU, and buffer pool resources.

children.title: Invocation
children.summary: This section explains that the Data Manager is started with the build database command. It notes a DB2 development optimization script that can speed table recreation during iterative builds by disabling dropped table recovery, but warns not to use it on production databases. It also introduces database artifacts, stating that the Data Manager uses generated and handcrafted artifacts to set up the database, beginning with Data Definition XML files that define tables and constraints.

children.title: Database artifacts
Data definition XML files
children.summary: Describes Data definition XML files (.xml) used to define database tables and related metadata. Shows sample XML structures for table definitions, foreign keys, primary keys, indices, unique constraints, batch metadata, and security/FID metadata, including key attributes like tablename, ddltype, notnull, size, and relationship fields. Also notes that DB2 for z/OS foreign keys may require manual DBA intervention to move tables out of check_pending. Useful for understanding how the Data Manager models schema and generated metadata.

children.title: Data contents DMX files
The table element
The <attribute> element
children.summary: Describes the DMX `<attribute>` element inside a `<row>`. It has required `name` and optional `encoding` (default UTF-8). Each attribute must contain a required `<value>` subelement. Notes that attribute order must match column order; names are not used during DMX processing, only the column definition order. Also explains `<value>` localization with `language` and `country`, warns primary/composite keys must not be localized, and lists valid value types by column type (`number`, `text`, `bool`, `id`, `blob`).

Explains the DMX `<table>` element for data contents files and its attributes. The table has required `name` and optional `override` (defaults false). It also documents the related `<column>`, `<row>`, `<attribute>`, and `<value>` elements, including required/optional attributes, UTF-8 default encoding, row localization, and that attribute order must match column order because names are ignored during processing. Notes `<value>` localization via language/country, forbids localizing primary/composite keys, and lists valid values by column type.

children.title: How to customize a DMX file
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram DMX files by adding new DMX files under SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/data/initial, demo, or test. Covers supported customizations (add, update, remove, localize, override), merge precedence via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, and the output location of merged files. Details merge rules for tables, rows, remove attributes, value inheritance, locale-specific values, and row locales. Includes examples showing table-level override, row removal, locale merging, and locale-scoped rows.

children.title: Retrieving values from DMX files for database insertion
children.summary: Explains how the Data Manager reads DMX files to decide what database values to insert. Covers row-level `remove` (skips insertion when true), the `curam.dmx.locale` setting (from Bootstrap.properties or system variable, with system variable overriding), and locale matching rules for `<row>` and `<value>` elements. Also describes precedence of row locales over attribute locales and fallback behavior when an exact locale match is missing. Includes an example DMX table showing locale-specific values.

children.title: Validation of DMX files
children.summary: This section explains DMX file validation during the build database target. DMX files in component data directories are checked against DMX.xsd, with warnings shown for format issues. Validation is enabled by default and can be disabled with curam.dmx.disable.validation=true. It also describes prp.warningstoerrors, which turns warnings into build errors. The section begins DMX tracing support, including curam.dmx.tracing=true to log file processing, duplicate-row key values, and completion messages.

children.title: Tracing Information for the DMX Merging Process
children.summary: Explains how to enable tracing for the DMX merging process using the `curam.dmx.tracing` system property, which is `false` by default. When set to `true`, it outputs console tracing to help debug DMX merge issues, including the file name being processed, key values for rows being merged when duplicates exist, and a completion message for each DMX file.

Explains DMX data contents files used to seed sample/test database data. Covers `<table>`, `<column>`, `<row>`, `<attribute>`, and `<value>` syntax and attributes, including required names, data types, UTF-8 defaults, locale fields, and ordering rules. Describes how to customize/override and merge DMX files across components via `SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER`, plus row removal and locale merging. Also explains how `curam.dmx.locale` controls locale selection and fallback during insertion, and how DMX files are schema-validated and traced during build database processing.

Explains Database artifacts used by Data Manager to build the database: generated/handcrafted XML DDL metadata and DMX sample data files. Covers table, foreign key, primary key, index, unique constraint, batch, security/FID, and field-level security XML structures, including DB2 for z/OS check_pending caveat. For DMX files, describes table/column/row/attribute/value syntax, customization via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, row removal, locale selection and fallback with curam.dmx.locale, primary-key-based merging, schema validation, and tracing during build database.

children.title: Database Object Naming
(deprecated) Short Name Substitution
children.summary: This section notes that Short Name Substitution is deprecated and will be removed in a future Cúram version because supported databases no longer need it; contact Cúram Support if still required. It also covers database object naming options for primary key indices and constraints, including default naming, optional prefixes via generator options, and SQL identifier length limits that can cause processing errors.

children.title: Primary key indices
children.summary: Describes primary key index naming in Cúram Data Manager. By default, a primary key index uses the same name as its table, but a prefix can be added with `-primarykeyindexprefix` via `extra.generator.options` (for example, `PI_Person`). Warns that exceeding the database SQL identifier length limit causes SQL processing errors. The page also begins the related topic of primary key constraints, including optional named constraints and a separate prefix option.

children.title: Primary key constraints
children.summary: Explains how Cúram generates primary key DDL and how to customize names. By default, primary keys are added with `alter table ... add primary key (...)`; using `-usenamedprimarykeyconstraint` changes this to `add constraint <name> primary key (...)`. The constraint name defaults to the table name, and can be prefixed with `-primarykeyconstraintprefix`. It warns that overly long names can exceed database SQL identifier limits and cause SQL processing errors.

children.title: Automatic index generation
children.summary: Describes automatic index generation during model generation: enabled by default, it adds indexes for non-indexed fields used in Readmulti, NsModify, NsRead, NsReadmulti, and NsRemove operations when the field is SVR_INT64, SVR_DATE, SVR_DATETIME, or SVR_STRING (excluding codetable domains and strings over 255 chars). Generated indexes use the name format IND_Hashcode of Table + FieldName. Also explains how to disable it with a server build parameter. Includes DB2 z/OS tablespace exceptions and Tablespace.properties behavior.

children.title: Tablespaces
children.summary: DB2 for z/OS tablespace handling during table creation. By default, tablespaces are created implicitly, except for the tablespace named by curam.db.zos.32ktablespace, which the datamanager creates explicitly for tables exceeding the 4K row limit, and tablespaces listed in Tablespace.properties, which are also created explicitly. If such tables exceed 4K row limits, they use the 32K BUFFERPOOL; otherwise default settings apply. The file maps tablename=tablespacename and uses # for comments.

Cúram’s database object naming covers how Data Manager names objects not explicit in XML. It notes deprecated Short Name Substitution, primary key index naming and optional prefixes, named primary key constraints via generator options, and SQL identifier length limits that can cause processing errors. It also explains automatic index generation for certain non-indexed fields used in specific operations, its default behavior, how to disable it, and DB2 z/OS tablespace exceptions and `Tablespace.properties` behavior.

children.title: Data Manager configuration
children.summary: Explains Data Manager configuration via `SERVER_DIR\project\config\datamanager_config.xml`. Covers the XML structure (`compositetarget`, `target`, `entry`) and entry attributes (`name`, `type` = sql/DMX/xml, `base` = basedir or sdescripts). Describes how build database uses the file, how to choose a composite target with `-Dprm.target` (default `all`), and how to add new composite targets/subtargets.

children.title: Database Synchronization
children.summary: Explains database synchronization utilities in Cúram: generating Data Contents XML/DMX files from the database via the `build extractdata` Ant target, optionally for specific tables, with extracted CLOB/BLOB contents saved separately under `%SERVER_DIR%/build/dataextractor`. Also covers the `runstatistics` target for gathering table/index statistics to help the optimizer choose access paths, noting it is unsupported on DB2 for z/OS. Includes LOB Manager basics for loading BLOB/CLOB data by referencing external files in data contents.

children.title: Statistics
children.summary: This section covers database maintenance and data loading support in Cúram. It explains how to generate Data Contents XML/DMX files from the database using the `build extractdata` Ant target, including optional table filtering and where extracted files and LOB contents are stored. It also describes the `runstatistics` target for collecting database optimizer statistics to improve access paths and avoid performance issues, noting DB2 for z/OS is not supported. Finally, it introduces the LOB Manager and how CLOB/BLOB values reference external files in data contents.

children.title: LOB Manager
children.summary: Explains the LOB Manager in Cúram’s Data Manager for loading CLOB and BLOB data from files referenced in Data Contents XML rather than literal values. Shows examples for BLOB and CLOB tables, including per-row and column-level UTF-16 encoding for CLOBs. Notes a key restriction: BLOB loading requires known primary key fields because it uses an insert followed by an update to target a single row.

Data Manager explains how Cúram builds and loads a new database from database-independent XML/DMX artifacts, not for upgrading existing databases. It covers the intended workflow, MBCS expansion settings in Bootstrap.properties, invocation via build database, and generated/handcrafted database artifacts. It also describes object naming and automatic indexes, tablespace exceptions for DB2 z/OS, datamanager_config.xml targets, extractdata and runstatistics utilities, and LOB Manager loading of BLOB/CLOB files with primary-key requirements and encoding options.

children.title: SQL Checker
Under the Hood
children.summary: Explains the SQL Checker for Cúram SDEJ JDBC SQL validation. It generates an Ant-driven temporary SQLJ program (/build/sqlcheck/SQLJTemp.sql) from handcrafted model SQL and Data Manager code, allowing syntax/semantic checks before deployment if the database is already created and populated. It also checks database portability, including host-variable-to-NULL comparisons, which may require rebuilding the .ear for a different database type. Notes that it reduces but does not eliminate deployment errors and is not a substitute for full testing.

children.title: Limitations
children.summary: The SQL Checker helps validate JDBC/SQLJ syntax, semantics, and some database portability issues before deployment, including checks like host-variable comparisons to NULL. It is generated as a static SQLJ program from the handcrafted SQL and Data Manager code, and can be used when the database is already created and populated. Limitation: it reduces deployment-time errors but is not a substitute for a full test suite and does not catch all possible errors.

Describes the SQL Checker for Cúram SDEJ JDBC SQL validation. It generates an Ant-driven temporary SQLJ program (/build/sqlcheck/SQLJTemp.sql) from handcrafted model SQL and Data Manager code to check SQL syntax, semantics, and some database portability issues before deployment, assuming the database is already created and populated. It also flags host-variable-to-NULL portability concerns that may require rebuilding the .ear for another database type. It reduces deployment errors but does not replace full testing.

Cúram application build/configuration overview. Covers server-side directory structure and component precedence via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER and locale prepending, build files/Ant targets and options, runtime configuration settings (Application.prx, Bootstrap.properties, time zone/date handling), Data Manager for creating/loading databases from XML/DMX with config targets, extractdata/statistics/LOB loading, and SQL Checker for validating handcrafted JDBC SQL via generated SQLJ, including portability checks and limits.

children.title: 1.2 SDEJ development and application programming interfaces
Eclipse
Cúram projects to import into Eclipse
children.summary: Explains which four Cúram projects must be imported into Eclipse for SDEJ development. It identifies CuramSDEJ (server development libraries), CuramCDEJ (client development libraries, depends on CuramSDEJ), and EJBServer (Cúram server application, depends on CuramSDEJ), and notes that Eclipse terminology applies to supported Eclipse-based tools such as Rational Application Developer. This section helps users set up the development workspace correctly.

children.title: Eclipse configuration files
.project file
children.summary: Explains the Eclipse `.project` file in Cúram: it stores project natures, builders, and dependencies. For the Curam project, it includes Java and Apache Tomcat natures to indicate deployability on Tomcat. Contrasts `.project` with `.classpath`, which defines source/target compilation paths and JAR/project dependencies. Also notes that `.classpath` for EJBServer and webclient can be generated via the `build createClasspaths` target from EJBServer, ideally added to the default build to keep paths environment-specific.

children.title: The .classpath file
Eclipse .classpath generation
children.summary: Explains that Eclipse .classpath files for the EJBServer and webclient projects can be generated by the build target `build createClasspaths` run from the EJBServer directory. This tailors classpaths to the local environment and avoids manual maintenance. It recommends adding this target to the default build wrapper (for example, `EJBServer\.build.bat`) so it runs with each build, though regeneration only occurs when environment changes require it.

Explains Eclipse project configuration files, focusing on .classpath. The .classpath stores source/target Java compilation paths and compressed-file or project dependencies, managed via the Java Build Path in project properties. It also notes that Eclipse .classpath files for the EJBServer and webclient projects can be generated with the build target `build createClasspaths` from the EJBServer directory, tailoring them to the local environment and reducing manual maintenance; this target is recommended for inclusion in the default build wrapper.

children.title: .settings directory
children.summary: The .settings directory stores Eclipse project-level preferences such as compiler warnings/errors and code style, especially for pages with “Enable project specific settings.” It can be checked into source control so team members share settings.

Explains Eclipse project configuration in Cúram: each project uses .project and .classpath files, plus optional project-level settings stored under .settings for team sharing. .project defines natures, builders, and dependencies; Curam includes an Apache Tomcat nature for deployment. .classpath manages Java source/target paths and JAR/project dependencies via Java Build Path. For EJBServer and webclient, .classpath can be generated with the build createClasspaths target from EJBServer to match the local environment and reduce manual maintenance.

children.title: Access Rules option
children.summary: Explains Eclipse .classpath access rules for compressed files: non-accessible, discouraged, and accessible. The compiler reports problem markers, typically errors for non-accessible and warnings for discouraged accesses, instead of compile failures. These rules support Cúram compliance by marking API-compliant use of JARs, but only apply to compressed files and do not fully enforce compliancy. The section warns that non-accessible/discouraged classes are unsupported and may change, impacting upgrades; discouraged accesses should be treated as non-accessible in custom code.

children.title: Working Sets
children.summary: Explains Eclipse Working Sets as a way to narrow navigation in large workspaces and focus views on selected code areas. Lists supported views/dialogs (Navigator, Package Explorer, Projects, Packages, Types, Problems, Open Type) and gives a step-by-step example of filtering the Problems View by creating and applying a Java working set. Also notes Cúram logging uses the `curam.util.resources.Trace` wrapper over Apache log4j 2.

Explains Eclipse use in Cúram SDEJ: the four projects to import (CuramSDEJ, CuramCDEJ, EJBServer, Curam/webclient) and their dependencies. Describes Eclipse project configuration via .project, .classpath, and optional .settings, including Tomcat nature and Java Build Path management. Notes .classpath can be generated by build createClasspaths for EJBServer/webclient. Also covers Access Rules for compressed files (accessible, discouraged, non-accessible) and how they mark warnings/errors to guide API-compliant development.

children.title: Logging that uses Apache log4j 2 API
Logging usage
children.summary: Explains how to use Cúram logging via the Log4j2-based tracing API, including the `Logger` interface, the top-level logger (`Trace.kTopLevelLogger`), and examples of logging messages and struct objects with `Trace.objectAsTraceString`. Recommends combining logically related data into a single trace call to avoid clutter. Introduces the logging hierarchy and categories/levels used by Cúram infrastructure for filtering trace records in Log4j2 viewers.

children.title: Logging hierarchy
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s logging hierarchy for trace records and how developers use the Log4j `Logger` via `curam.util.resources.Trace` to emit logs. It describes that logs can go to an app-server file, standalone file, console, or database, and recommends combining logically related data into one trace call. It also introduces the categorized/leveled trace records used by the infrastructure, which help filter logs in Log4j 2 viewers, including top-level and class-based categories.

children.title: Logging trace levels
children.summary: Explains Cúram server logging trace levels and how to use them to guard Log4j 2 calls for better performance when tracing is disabled. Shows how to check the current setting with `curam.util.resources.Trace.atLeast(Trace t)`, using `Trace.kTraceOff` or `Trace.kTraceOn`. Also includes a logging hierarchy table listing categories such as BatchLauncher, CodeTable, DataAccess, Methods, Rules, ServerCalls, and Tools with their Info/Debug/Error meanings.

children.title: Configuring the Apache Log4j 2 logging utility
children.summary: Explains how to configure Cúram’s logging via Apache Log4j 2 using the curam.trace.configfile.location property, with examples for file and socket appenders. Covers the default log4j2.xml behavior (console output, DEBUG root level), a rolling file configuration, and a socket configuration for monitoring tools like Chainsaw. Also describes log pattern elements, rollover settings, and how Cúram trace/user properties affect what gets logged, including scenarios for online and deferred process transactions.

children.title: Logging statistics
children.summary: Describes Cúram server performance statistics logging for client-visible functions, as a lower-overhead alternative to tracing. Statistics are written per session bean to separate tab-delimited log files with 4 KB buffers, in a directory controlled by `curam.test.trace.statistics.location` when `curam.test.trace.statistics` is enabled. Files are named `<MachineName>_<SessionBeanName>_0.<TimeStamp>`. Each record includes sortable ISO 8601 timestamp, machine name, session bean name, process ID, and server function signature.

children.title: Localization of log messages
children.summary: Explains how to localize log messages with `LocalisableString` and why logged messages should use the Cúram server’s default locale, not the current user’s locale. Shows calling `getMessage(ProgramLocale.getDefaultServerLocale())` to ensure logs are written in the server locale. Also notes command-line display of non-English localized text depends on the system locale. Ends by introducing dynamic UIM tracing, which is off by default and requires `Tracing Level=trace_on` or higher and `Enable tracing of Dynamic UI=true`.

children.title: How to enable dynamic UIM tracing
children.summary: Explains how to turn on dynamic UIM tracing, which is off by default. To log missing Dynamic User Interface Metadata (UIM) resources, set Tracing Level to trace_on or higher and Enable tracing of Dynamic UI to true. Useful for troubleshooting dynamic UI metadata logging and related configuration.

Explains Cúram logging via the Log4j2-based `curam.util.resources.Trace` API, including top-level and category-based loggers, `objectAsTraceString`, and recommended single-call logging for related data. Defines trace hierarchy/levels and `Trace.atLeast(...)` guards, plus standard trace properties for server calls, methods, SQL, rules, and SMTP. Covers Log4j2 configuration via `curam.trace.configfile.location`, file/socket appenders, and default console DEBUG behavior. Also describes lower-overhead server statistics logging, locale-correct log messages, and enabling dynamic UIM tracing.

children.title: How to use exceptions
Constructing an exception
children.summary: Explains how exceptions are constructed and mapped for clients in Cúram. Most unchecked exceptions are replaced by a new “Unhandled” exception that nests the original details (catalog name, message ID, arguments), except RecordChangedException, RecordDeletedException, RecordLockedException, and ReadmultiMaxException, which are passed through. Shows creating an AppException with a catalog/message ID, including default values, and attaching extra information with arg() so the client or server log can format the final localized message.

children.title: Creating messages with argument placeholders
children.summary: Explains how AppException `arg()` adds runtime arguments to exceptions, including primitive and complex types like Date, Money, and CodeTableItemIdentifier. Introduces message placeholders in error text using `%nc` tokens, where `n` is the argument number and `c` indicates type (`s`, `n`, `d`, `t`, `z`, `c`). Shows that placeholders are replaced with actual values at runtime and can appear in any order.

children.title: Handling exceptions
children.summary: Explains how exceptions are handled in Cúram applications, especially in try..catch blocks. Exceptions may be ignored and processing continued, rethrown to a higher handler, or replaced by a new application exception with a more specific message. Gives examples such as treating RecordNotFoundException as “record does not exist,” selectively handling a known AppException, and passing unknown exceptions upward. Also shows how to construct and throw an AppException with argument placeholders.

children.title: Logging exceptions
children.summary: Explains how to make exceptions loggable with setLoggable so they are written to the application log by the RIL. The logged message comes from the error catalog, with arguments substituted, and any attached exceptions are also logged. It also notes that an exception is considered loggable if its own flag or any attached exception’s flag is set. Includes general exception guidance: follow method specs, avoid catch-all handlers, add context when rethrowing, and understand standard/core exceptions and database-generated exceptions.

children.title: General exception guidelines
children.summary: Guidelines for handling exceptions in Cúram server code: follow each method’s processing specification, avoid catch-all handlers and wrapping whole operations, and let server infrastructure handle unanticipated errors. Add handling only when you can provide more specific context, such as converting “record not found” into a domain-specific error. Also understand standard core exceptions and the exceptions generated by entity object database operations.

children.title: Coding Conventions for Exceptions
children.summary: This section lists which database/entity operations can throw each exception, including RecordNotFound, RecordDeleted, DuplicateRecord, RecordChanged, MultipleRecord, ReadmultiMax, RecordLocked, and OtherDatabaseException, with notes on precedence and optimistic locking behavior. It then gives coding conventions for exceptions: avoid creating custom AppException/AppRuntimeException subclasses, use exception chaining and logging for serious errors, and avoid hard-coded literals in message text due to localization concerns. An example shows incorrect handling of a RecordNotFoundException with a non-localizable string.

children.title: How to use the Record Not Found indicator
children.summary: Explains how to use `curam.util.type.NotFoundIndicator` with singleton database reads (`entity read`, `nsread`, `nkread`) that may otherwise throw `RecordNotFoundException`. The indicator is passed as the first argument; it avoids exceptions for missing records, sets its boolean flag to true when not found, false when found, and returns null if absent. The section also notes benefits: lower JVM exception overhead and easier existence checks, with examples for normal read and read-for-update overloads.

children.title: Localized output
children.summary: Explains when to use `curam.util.exception.LocalisableString` to return server-generated text for client-side localization, typically by building a localized string and passing it back as output. Also introduces the `InformationalManager`, which groups multiple warnings/errors or informational messages so they can be presented together, especially for validation scenarios with independent issues. Includes example code showing how these classes are used and notes that a transaction-wide informational manager is available via `TransactionInfo.getInformationalManager()`.

children.title: Use of the Informational Manager
children.summary: Explains when to use `curam.util.exception.LocalisableString` for server-side data that the client must localize, then introduces `InformationalManager` for grouping multiple warnings/errors to return together. Shows a login validation example using `InformationalManager`, adding two warning messages, converting them to strings, and packaging them into `LoginMessage`/`LoginMessageList` structs for client return. Useful for handling multiple informational messages in one response.

Explains Cúram exception use: checked vs unchecked exceptions, how the RIL logs and remaps them, and when to catch, rethrow, or replace errors with application-specific ones. Shows constructing AppException with catalog/message IDs, attaching typed arguments, and using `%nc` placeholders in localized messages. Covers exception chaining and loggable exceptions, general coding guidelines, database exception types, and the NotFoundIndicator for existence checks without exceptions. Also introduces LocalisableString and InformationalManager for returning localized or multiple informational messages.

children.title: Message files
The Format of Message Files
The <messages> Element
children.summary: The `<messages>` element is the root of a message file and groups all other elements. It has one required attribute, `package`, which specifies the Java package name for the generated Java file. This section sits within the message.xml format description and is useful if you need the file’s top-level structure or the purpose of the package attribute.

This section introduces the message .xml file format used by msggen, including how build scripts merge message files and generate Java symbol definitions and property-based message catalogs. It explains that message files are UTF-8 XML documents and shows a sample structure. It then describes the `<messages>` root element, which groups all content and requires the `package` attribute to specify the Java package for the generated file.

children.title: Customizing a Message File
Rules of Message Merges
children.summary: Explains how message files are merged by component precedence defined in SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, from highest to lowest priority, with core lowest. Any unspecified components are ordered alphabetically between specified components and core. Changing precedence requires a clean server build. Merge rules: add a <message> only if it does not already exist in the target file; add a <locale> only if that locale is absent for the existing message. Duplicate messages are overwritten by the higher-precedence component.

Explains how to customize message XML files by overriding component message files under SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/message without changing core, and warns that changing the package attribute breaks the override. Describes message-file merging by SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence: leftmost highest, core always lowest, unspecified components inserted alphabetically above core. Changing precedence requires a clean server build. Merge rules add only missing <message> elements and missing <locale> entries; duplicate messages are overwritten by higher-precedence components.

children.title: Artefacts Produced by msggen Build Target
children.summary: Describes the output of the msggen build target after merging message files. It generates Java artefacts (symbol definition and message catalog files) under build/svr/message/gen/<package>, with the package path derived from the message file’s package attribute (for example, curam.message becomes build/svr/message/gen/curam/message). The output includes locale-independent Java files and locale-specific .properties files named using the message file name plus language/country codes.

children.title: Retrieving Messages from Message Files
children.summary: Explains how localized messages are retrieved from message files at runtime. A message file can define multiple locales per message, and Cúram selects the correct message using the runtime locale’s language and optional country. It describes locale-based lookup for a named message identifier and notes that the generated property files are packaged into a JAR for client-side localization. This section is useful for understanding message localization behavior and lookup rules.

children.title: Writing Messages To Server Logs
children.summary: Explains how server log messages are localized differently from regular message lookups: logs use the server infrastructure’s default locale, configured via `curam.environment.default.locale` in `Application.prx`. Also covers localizing or modifying shipped Cúram SDEJ message files (`.iml`): copy the file from the SDEJ message directory into a component’s message directory (for example, `SERVER_DIR/components/custom/message`) and then override messages or add locales.

children.title: Localizing SDEJ Message Files
children.summary: Explains how to localize or override Cúram SDEJ message files. SDEJ messages are stored in the SDEJ message directory as .iml files, in the same format as application message files. To customize them, copy the target .iml file into a Cúram application component’s message directory (for example, SERVER_DIR/components/custom/message) and edit it there. You can override existing messages or add new locale variants.

Defines Cúram message files and localization flow. Covers UTF-8 XML `.xml`/`.iml` message catalogs, `<messages>`/`<message>`/`<locale>` structure, and `SERVER_LOCALE_LIST` supported locales. Explains overriding shipped messages by copying files into `SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/message`, with merge precedence from `SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER` and a clean build required after changes. Describes `msggen` outputs: Java symbol classes plus locale-specific `.properties` under `build/svr/message/gen/<package>`, packaged into a JAR. Also details runtime locale lookup, fallback rules, server log default locale, and SDEJ message localization.

children.title: Code table files
The code table file format
children.summary: Defines the XML format for Cúram code table files. Covers root `<codetables>` attributes (`package`, optional `hierarchy_name`), optional `<description>`, `<codetable>` and its attributes (`name`, `java_identifier`, optional `parent_codetable`), locale-specific `<codetabledata>`, optional `<displaynames>` with `<name>`/`<locale>`, `<code>` attributes (`value`, `status`, `default`, `java_identifier`, `removed`, `parent_code`), code `<locale>` contents, and optional `<views>`/`<view>` structures for application-context-specific code display and merging behavior.

children.title: Customizing a code table file
Rules of code table merges
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram code table files by overriding included files in SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/codetable and merging same-named files according to SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence. Defines merge rules for <code>, <codetabledata>, <locale>, <displaynames>, <name>, <views>, and <view>, including when entries are added, merged, or overwritten. Shows worked examples of merged code tables, illustrating locale additions, new codes, display name merging by language/country, and view merging/overwrite behavior.

Explains how to customize Cúram code table files by overriding included files in SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/codetable instead of editing core files. Describes merge precedence via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER and the rules for merging <code>, <codetabledata>, <locale>, <displaynames>, <name>, <views>, and <view> elements, including add/merge/overwrite behavior and default_code handling. Includes worked XML examples showing locale additions, new codes, merged display names by language/country, and view merging versus overwrite.

children.title: Artifacts produced by the ctgen build target
children.summary: The ctgen build target generates two artifacts from code table XML: a Java symbol definition file and an SQL data file. The Java class contains constants for table and code identifiers plus helper accessors, and is written under /build/svr/codetable/gen/<package>. The SQL file is written to /build/svr/codetable/sql/ and populates CodeTableHeader, CodeTableItem, CodeTableView, and CodeTableViewCode. It also notes component precedence for duplicate names, locale filtering via SERVER_LOCALE_LIST, and that LASTWRITTEN is timestamped at generation.

children.title: Code table hierarchy
children.summary: Explains how to define and generate hierarchical code tables in a CTX file. Covers properties-file generation for display names, locale handling, warnings for missing/unsupported `<displaynames>` locales, and filename normalization of spaces. Describes hierarchy setup using `hierarchy_name`, `parent_codetable`, and `parent_code`, with rules that each code table/child code can have only one parent. Includes a sample Country → County → Town XML hierarchy and notes that the usual ctgen artefacts are also produced for hierarchies.

children.title: Retrieving locale codes from code table files
children.summary: Explains how Cúram resolves localized codes from code table (CTX) files at runtime. A named code can have multiple locale-specific descriptions; lookup uses the runtime locale’s language and optional country (e.g., en_US returns “The code”, fr returns “Le code”). Includes an example XML code table with locale entries and notes that if no exact locale match exists, fallback rules apply to find the best available localized code.

children.title: Localizing SDEJ code table files
children.summary: Explains how SDEJ-shipped code table files can be localized by copying .itx files into an application component’s codetable directory and overriding or adding locales.
