Notes that localization works only if the package attribute is unchanged, and that ctgen merges the localized file with the original SDEJ file, giving the localized version precedence and generating SQL only (no Java artifacts). Also notes datamanager_config.xml usually needs no update, and clarifies the optional CTX <description> behavior, especially for Code Table Hierarchies.

Defines Cúram code table files (CTX/ITX): XML structure, required attributes, locale-specific descriptions/comments, display names, and optional views. Explains generation by ctgen into Java symbol definitions and SQL artifacts, and how code table hierarchies are built with hierarchy_name, parent_codetable, and parent_code. Covers runtime locale lookup and fallback, and how to localize shipped SDEJ code tables by overriding .itx files in custom components without changing package names.

children.title: Specialized readmulti operations
When to Use Readmulti Operations
children.summary: Explains when to replace standard readmulti operations with specialized processing. Use them for batch processing over many database records, when you need to process rows one at a time, or when locking/deleting records as they are read. Standard readmulti returns all records as an ArrayList, which may be impractical for large result sets. Also introduces the structure of readmulti operations: Data Access Layer plus Business Object Layer, implemented via ReadMultiOperation and StandardReadMultiOperation helper classes.

children.title: How to define your own readmulti operations
children.summary: This section explains how to define custom specialized readmulti operations instead of using standard `StandardReadMultiOperation`. It describes readmulti operations as a DAL function plus a BOL function, and notes they can be used for custom cursor processing such as batch iteration, locking, or deleting records as they are read. It then begins the procedure: add the readmulti operation to the UML application model, then implement a specialized class extending `curam.util.dataaccess.ReadmultiOperation`.

children.title: Extra features of readmulti operations
children.summary: Explains advanced customization points for hand-crafted readmulti operations. Covers how READMULTI_MAX does not apply and must be replaced via getMaximum(), how to filter rows with filter(), and lifecycle hooks pre(), first(), and post(). Notes that post() always runs, first() runs if any row exists, and an optional helper parameter can lock read rows for later update. Includes an example grouping records and producing department/grand totals using overridden methods and state fields.

children.title: An alternative
children.summary: Describes an alternative to specialized readmulti/non-standard operations when query logic is too dynamic for fixed SQL: obtain the transaction’s Connection via TransactionInfo.getInfoConnection and execute handcrafted JDBC within the Cúram transaction. Advises placing such access in a NO_SQL entity to expose it in the model. Also reiterates readmulti method order: pre, first, filter, operation, post, and getMaximum, with a note to consider lock performance impacts.

children.title: Summary
children.summary: This section summarizes readmulti operation sequencing: pre runs once first, then first on the initial record if any, filter on every record, operation on filtered records, post once at the end, and getMaximum controls the number of matched records. It also notes that specialized readmulti/non-standard operations can be extended with handcrafted JDBC via TransactionInfo.getInfoConnection, and that such database access should be modeled with the NO_SQL option. A warning is given about lock-maintaining processing and performance.

Introduces specialized readmulti operations as handcrafted DAL/BOL processing for SQL cursors, used when standard ArrayList-based readmulti is impractical (large batches, row-by-row processing, locking/deleting). Explains how to define one by adding it to the UML model, extending `ReadmultiOperation`, and invoking generated helper classes. Covers customization hooks `getMaximum`, `filter`, `pre`, `first`, `post`, plus optional row-locking and memory cautions with Post Data Access/On-fail. Also describes a JDBC alternative via `TransactionInfo.getInfoConnection` and `NO_SQL`, and summarizes call order.

children.title: Deprecation
Overview
Other Sources of Information
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram uses deprecation to manage product change. It defines deprecation in code and documentation, notes that deprecated artifacts remain supported but won’t be enhanced and may be removed later, and states removal can occur only in a later V.R.M. release at least 12 months after deprecation. It also points readers to where to find deprecation details for specific artifacts: the artifact itself and the “Notes on Deprecation” in release notes.

Overview of Cúram deprecation: why features/APIs are deprecated, how deprecation works in code and documentation, and what it means for support and future removal. Deprecated items remain supported but won’t be enhanced and may be removed in a later V.R.M. release, at least 12 months after deprecation. Deprecation can be marked in code, documentation, or both. It also points to where to find details on specific deprecated artifacts: the artifact itself and the “Notes on Deprecation” in release notes.

children.title: Effect of Deprecation on a Custom Application
Customizations and References
children.summary: Explains how deprecation affects custom Cúram applications, distinguishing references from customizations. References to deprecated artifacts usually keep working backward-compatibly, while customizations/overrides may stop functioning because deprecated artifacts are no longer in the base application’s default flow. Lists examples (server methods, workflows, UIM pages) and notes build/validation warnings include deprecation comments, with extra context in release notes. Useful for assessing whether custom code depends on deprecated artifacts and needs remediation.

children.title: Support for Deprecated artifacts
children.summary: Deprecated artifacts remain supported until removed in a future release, but customers should address dependencies early to avoid compilation and upgrade issues. The section explains how deprecation affects the user interface: replacement pages may change default flows and require customization updates, while unreplaced pages have their links removed. It also notes impacts on documentation/training materials and describes how customers can reinstate deprecated functionality by overriding configuration and re-adding links.

children.title: Effect of Deprecation on the User Interface
children.summary: Deprecating client pages changes the default UI flow to use replacement pages or remove links entirely if no replacement exists. This can affect consistency with custom pages and may require updating customizations, documentation, screenshots, and training materials. Customers can reinstate removed links by overriding application configuration files and re-adding links, often using a diff utility. The section also notes that deprecated artifacts remain supported while present, but should be addressed early to avoid future compilation failures and upgrade issues.

children.title: Reinstating Deprecated Functionality
children.summary: Explains how to reinstate links for deprecated features that were removed from the UI by overriding application configuration files and re-adding the links, with diff tools recommended for customers upgrading from earlier releases. Also notes that deprecated artifacts remain supported until removal, but custom dependencies should be updated early to avoid future compilation failures. Mentions UI impacts of deprecating client pages, including flow changes, replacement pages, and the need to update custom documentation/training materials.

Explains how deprecation affects custom Cúram applications. Deprecated artifacts stay in the codebase and remain backward-compatible for references, but customizations/overrides may stop working because they’re removed from the default runtime flow. Gives examples for server methods, workflows, and UIM pages, and notes build/validation warnings include deprecation comments and release-note references. Useful for checking whether custom code depends on deprecated artifacts and may need remediation, especially for UI flow changes or reinstating removed links.

children.title: Scope
Artifact types that can be deprecated
children.summary: Lists which artifact types can be deprecated in Cúram and how deprecation is marked across model, Java, JavaScript, client UI, configuration, messages, DMX, rules, properties, events, and workflow artifacts. Notes that deprecated artifacts remain supported until removed, and warns about UI impacts when client pages are deprecated. Explains special handling for entities: they are not tagged deprecated in the model because DDL generation would be affected, but may still appear in release notes and should be treated as deprecated for dependency removal.

children.title: Limitations of the deprecation infrastructure
children.summary: Describes limitations and setup for Cúram deprecation reporting. Key limits: build warnings do not catch non-typed references (for example, string literals to deprecated UIM pages), and deprecation tagging is unsupported for customer use (e.g., `<?curam-deprecated` in custom XML). Also explains how to run the Deprecation Report: `prp.warningstoerrors` must be false, `curam.deprecation.reporting` controls warning display, sample components should be removed to avoid noise, prerequisite builds must succeed, and report output is logged to `%SERVER_DIR%\buildlogs\%Deprecation<timestamp>.log`.

Lists which Cúram artifact types can be deprecated and how deprecation is marked across modeled artifacts, Java/JavaScript, client UI files, configuration, messages, DMX, rules, properties, events, and workflow definitions. Notes that all deprecated artifacts remain supported until removed, but client page deprecation can alter UI flow and documentation. Explains entity classes are treated specially: they are not tagged deprecated in the model because that would affect DDL generation, but may still be listed in release notes and should be treated as deprecated for dependency removal.

children.title: Running a Deprecation Report
Configuring the Deprecation Report
children.summary: This section explains how to configure and run Cúram deprecation reporting. It covers key properties: set prp.warningstoerrors to false, and use curam.deprecation.reporting in bootstrap.properties to enable or suppress warnings. It also recommends removing Sample components from CLIENT_COMPONENT_ORDER to avoid noisy warnings. Prerequisites include successful build clean server, build clean client, and build database runs before deprecationreport. The section also notes limitations: only typed references are detected, and deprecation tagging (e.g., <?curam-deprecated) is not supported for customer use.

children.title: Prerequisites for running the Deprecation Report
children.summary: Lists the prerequisites for running the Cúram Deprecation Report. Before invoking the deprecationreport target, ensure build clean server, build clean client, and build database complete successfully, since the report depends on these steps. It also notes deprecation reporting is controlled by build properties, and warns that sample components may create irrelevant warnings. The section belongs to the broader guidance on deprecation infrastructure limitations and generating deprecation build output.

children.title: Generating the Deprecation build output
children.summary: Explains how to generate Cúram deprecation build output for analysis. It notes limitations of deprecation infrastructure: only typed references produce warnings, and the deprecation tagging mechanism itself is not supported for customer use. It describes configuration requirements for deprecation reporting (set prp.warningstoerrors=false, curam.deprecation.reporting=true by default) and recommends removing Sample components from CLIENT_COMPONENT_ORDER. Before running deprecationreport, build clean server, build clean client, and build database must succeed. Output is captured in %SERVER_DIR%\buildlogs\%Deprecation<timestamp>.log.

children.title: Identifying deprecation warnings in the build output
children.summary: Explains how to read the deprecation build output after running `build deprecationreport`. Check `Deprecation<timestamp>.log` for `BUILD SUCCESSFUL` or `BUILD FAILED`, then filter for `[deprecation]` to create a `deprecation_summary.log` of warning messages. Notes that some warnings span multiple lines and that the original log should be kept. Also covers runtime notes: the report can take time, validation can be run separately if it fails, and the target may fail if validation errors prevent complete build output.

children.title: Notes on running the Deprecation Report
children.summary: This section explains how to run the deprecation report build target, check the Deprecation<timestamp>.log for BUILD SUCCESSFUL/FAILED, and extract deprecation warnings with grep or find into deprecation_summary.log. It notes the build can take time because it does a clean build plus server/client builds and validations. It also explains failure handling: validation issues can be run separately, but deprecationreport depends on validation and may fail if prerequisite build output is missing.

Explains how to run Cúram’s deprecation report and interpret its output. Covers limitations of the deprecation infrastructure: only typed references trigger warnings, and deprecation tagging such as `<?curam-deprecated` is unsupported for customer use. Describes required settings (`prp.warningstoerrors=false`, `curam.deprecation.reporting` in `bootstrap.properties`) and recommends removing Sample components from `CLIENT_COMPONENT_ORDER`. Requires successful `build clean server`, `build clean client`, and `build database` before `build deprecationreport`, which writes `Deprecation<timestamp>.log`; filter `[deprecation]` to create a summary log.

children.title: Analyzing Deprecation Warnings
Identifying overrides of deprecated artifacts
Addressing overrides of deprecated artifacts
children.summary: Explains how to handle overrides of deprecated artifacts: there is no single fix, so analyze the original customization and decide whether to drop it, reapply it to the replacement artifact, or otherwise adapt it. Also shows how to identify references to deprecated artifacts in deprecation logs via [deprecation] entries without [customization], and warns that deprecation messages may appear to originate from out-of-box UIMs when actually caused by custom VIMs. Notes on sample components, [deprecation comment], [bopigen], and generated Java warnings are included.

Explains how to identify overrides of deprecated artifacts in deprecation summary reports. Warnings tagged with both [deprecation] and [customization] indicate a custom artifact is overriding a now-deprecated Cúram artifact, unlike plain [deprecation] references. The section notes code generators and validations emit these warnings, includes the message format, and shows that attached [deprecation comment] text is printed when available to help determine the replacement or next action.

children.title: Identifying references to deprecated artifacts
children.summary: Explains how to identify references to deprecated artifacts in the deprecation log: lines tagged with [deprecation] but not [customization]. Shows an example warning for a deprecated UIM page and notes that such warnings indicate the referenced artifact is deprecated and may no longer be used in the default flow. Also covers caveats for interpreting warnings from overridden VIM files, sample components, [deprecation comment] markers, duplicate [bopigen] warnings, and generated Java warnings.

children.title: Notes on analyzing deprecation warnings
children.summary: Guidance for interpreting deprecation warnings in Cúram builds. It explains how to address overrides of deprecated artifacts by analyzing custom changes and either dropping them or reapplying them to replacement artifacts. It also shows how to identify direct references to deprecated artifacts via deprecation log entries without customization markers. Key cautions: out-of-the-box warnings may actually originate from custom VIM files used by UIM pages, sample components can emit warnings, [bopigen] warnings are duplicates, and generated Java warnings usually follow from customization issues.

Explains how to interpret deprecation summary logs in Cúram builds. Use [deprecation] [customization] warnings to find custom artifacts overriding deprecated Cúram artifacts, and plain [deprecation] warnings to find direct references. Shows message format and how [deprecation comment] text reveals replacements. Notes that out-of-the-box UIM warnings may actually come from custom VIM files, sample components can generate warnings, [bopigen] entries are duplicates, and generated Java warnings usually follow customization issues.

Explains Cúram deprecation: why artifacts/features are deprecated, that they remain supported but won’t be enhanced and may be removed in a later V.R.M. release (minimum 12 months later), and where to find deprecation notes/comments. Describes impact on custom apps, especially differences between references and overrides, and UI flow changes when client pages are deprecated. Lists deprecated artifact types, special handling for entities, and how to run/analyze deprecation reports and warnings to find custom dependencies.

children.title: User Preferences
User Preferences Definition
Data definition XML file
children.summary: Explains how to define or override user preferences via a custom `DefaultPreferences.xml` file in `EJBServer\components\<component_name>\userpreferences`. Describes the XML structure: a root `<Preferences>` element containing exactly one `<PreferenceSet id="default">`, which can include multiple `<Preference>` entries. Shows example fields such as `name`, `category`, `type`, `value`, `readonly`, `visible`, and `externalVisible`, and notes that users can edit preferences in the web client.

children.title: Properties files
children.summary: User preferences defined in `DefaultPreferences.xml` should also have matching `DefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties` entries. These properties files supply the display name shown in the web client’s user preference editor and can be localized per supported locale. If the locale-specific file does not exist, it should be created. This section explains the purpose of these accompanying properties files and points to localization details.

Explains how to define or override user preferences with a custom `DefaultPreferences.xml` under `EJBServer\components\<component_name>\userpreferences`. The file uses a root `<Preferences>` containing one `<PreferenceSet id="default">` with multiple `<Preference>` entries, each specifying fields like `name`, `type`, `value`, `readonly`, `visible`, and `externalVisible`. Also covers merging across components and recommends overriding only default values. A matching `DefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties` file provides localized display names for the web client editor.

children.title: Development support
children.summary: This section explains development-time access to user preferences via `curam.util.userpreference.impl.UserPreference.getValue()` and `setValue()`, noting preferences must be created first. It also covers enabling user preferences for external users: client-side `USER_PREFS_PAGE` on `<link>`, server-side `ExternalAccessSecurity` methods `getUserPreferenceSetID()` and `modifyUserPreferenceSetID()`, and controlling visibility with `<externalVisible>` in `DefaultPreferences.xml`. It also describes localizing display names with `DefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties` files under component `userpreferences` directories.

children.title: External users
children.summary: Explains how to expose user preferences to external users and localize their display names. Client changes require setting USER_PREFS_PAGE=true on a link. Server-side, ExternalAccessSecurity must implement getUserPreferenceSetID() and modifyUserPreferenceSetID(). Preference visibility is controlled by the <externalVisible> element in DefaultPreferences.xml; if false or missing, preferences won’t appear. Also describes creating DefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties files under EJBServer\components\<component_name>\userpreferences to provide localized preference labels.

children.title: Localizing display names
children.summary: Explains how to localize user preference display names by creating `DefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties` files alongside `DefaultPreferences.xml` in each component’s `userpreferences` directory. Files are merged into `MergedDefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties` using `SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER` during merge targets. Covers build-time validation rules for locale format, duplicates, empties, and invalid entries, and describes locale fallback matching (full locale, language-only, default) with an `fr_CA` example.

children.title: Localizing infrastructure preferences display names
children.summary: Explains how to localize infrastructure preference display names using `InfrastructurePreferences_<locale>.properties` files placed under `EJBServer\components\<component_name>\userpreferences`. Describes locale-based lookup behavior similar to user preferences, with fallback to the default `InfrastructurePreferences.properties` sample in `SDEJ\lib`. Also notes related application resource concepts and defines locales as language/country formatting settings used for i18n/l10n.

Covers user preferences in Cúram: out-of-box defaults, how to define or override preferences via `DefaultPreferences.xml` under `EJBServer\components\<component_name>\userpreferences`, required `<PreferenceSet id="default">` structure, and key fields like `name`, `type`, `value`, `readonly`, `visible`, and `externalVisible`. Explains component merge behavior and recommends overriding only default values. Also describes web-client editing, external user enablement via `USER_PREFS_PAGE` and `ExternalAccessSecurity`, plus localization using `DefaultPreferences_<locale>.properties` and locale fallback/validation rules.

Cúram SDEJ guidance on compliant development and API use. Covers Eclipse setup: importing CuramSDEJ, CuramCDEJ, EJBServer, and Curam/webclient; project files (.project, .classpath, .settings); Tomcat nature; classpath generation; and access rules for compressed libraries. Also outlines working sets, Log4j2-based tracing via `Trace`, deprecation handling and warnings, and user preferences: defining/overriding `DefaultPreferences.xml`, localization properties, external-user visibility, merge rules, and runtime access via `UserPreference`.

children.title: 1.3 Application Resources
Locale of Application Resource
children.summary: Explains how application resources are locale-aware and used server-side as blobs in AppResource for dynamic UIMs, properties, images, XML, and similar artifacts. Defines a locale as the user’s regional/cultural settings, typically language plus country (for example, en_US or fr_FR), used for internationalization and localization. Useful for understanding how resource localization is structured and how locale strings are interpreted in the application.

children.title: Fallback for properties files
children.summary: Describes locale fallback behavior for AppResource/property files. When retrieving a resource, the system searches fallback locales in order (for example, en_US → en → null) until a match is found. Null-locale resources act as the default and are available to all users, even when no locale-specific translation exists. If resources are stored only under a specific locale like en, users of other locales may see nothing; storing a null-locale version ensures a fallback is always displayed.

Application Resources are server-side artifacts (dynamic UIMs, properties, images, XML, CSS, etc.) stored as blobs in the AppResource entity and categorized by ResourceStoreCategory for uses like Display Rules and IEG. This section explains that application resources are locale-aware: locale strings encode language/country settings (for example, en_US, fr_FR) and support i18n/l10n. It’s useful for understanding how server-side resource artifacts are stored, categorized, and interpreted by locale.

children.title: 1.4 Cúram runtime behavior
Transaction control
Transactions and method invocations
children.summary: Explains how Cúram maps Facade method invocations to transactions: typically one transaction per method, beginning at invocation start and ending at completion, with exceptions for deferred processes/workflows and Key Server unique ID allocation. Notes transaction demarcation is bean-managed, so developers must use infrastructure APIs to checkpoint transactions. Also covers optimistic locking versus the forUpdate flag, which applies update locks on read and holds them until transaction end, reducing version checks but increasing lock/deadlock risk.

children.title: Optimistic locking and the forUpdate flag
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram maps Facade method calls to transactions, usually one transaction per method with bean-managed demarcation, plus a special separate transaction for Key Server unique ID blocks. It then introduces optimistic locking and when to use the forUpdate flag: passing true to standard read operations takes an update lock until transaction end, useful when a record is read then modified later to avoid version checks, but with increased risk of locks and deadlocks.

children.title: General guidelines
children.summary: Guidelines for transaction locking and performance: hold locks for the shortest possible time, defer validation/updates and any reads with locks until late in the transaction, and avoid long scans after locking. Explains server-side transaction management, consistency across client/server calls, and use of two-phase commit for multiple resource managers. Describes DB2 isolation behavior: non-forUpdate queries use Cursor Stability, forUpdate/modifies use Repeatable Read with row locks until commit. Notes Oracle uses its default isolation level and does not allow dirty reads.

children.title: Underlying design
DB2
children.summary: Explains transaction and locking guidelines for Cúram server DB access: keep locks short-lived, defer reads/updates with locks until late, and validate before updating. Describes server-side transaction management, distributed transactions, and two-phase commit across the database and deferred-processing/workflow queues. For DB2, Cúram uses one connection per transaction: queries without forUpdate run at Cursor Stability (WITH CS, read committed), while modifies and forUpdate queries use Repeatable Read with row locks held until commit. Oracle uses its default isolation level; dirty reads are not possible.

children.title: Oracle
children.summary: Explains transaction locking guidelines and server-side transaction design, including deferring locks/updates until late in a transaction and using two-phase commit across database and queue resources. For DB2, describes isolation behavior: non-forUpdate queries use Cursor Stability (Read Committed), while modifies and forUpdate queries use Repeatable Read with row locks held until commit. For Oracle, notes JDBC isolation levels are not truly supported, so Oracle’s default isolation level is used for all statements and dirty reads cannot occur.

This section explains Cúram’s transaction-management design and locking guidelines: keep locks short-lived, defer updates and reads with locks until late, and validate before modifying. It describes server-side transactions, not client-side, and the need for two-phase commit when a transaction spans the database plus deferred-processing/workflow queues. It also outlines DB2 isolation behavior (default connection with Cursor Stability for normal queries; Repeatable Read for modifies and forUpdate, with row locks held until commit) and notes Oracle uses its default isolation level, with dirty reads impossible.

Explains Cúram server transaction control: Facade methods usually run in one bean-managed transaction, with exceptions for deferred processing/workflows and Key Server unique ID allocation. Covers optimistic locking and the forUpdate flag, which takes update locks on reads until transaction end to reduce version checks but increases lock/deadlock risk. Gives locking/performance guidance: keep locks short, defer locked reads/updates, and use two-phase commit across database and deferred-processing/workflow resource managers. Also summarizes DB2 and Oracle isolation behavior.

children.title: Use of the transaction SQL query cache
How results get stored in the query cache
children.summary: Explains how the transaction SQL query cache works in Cúram SDEJ. It caches SELECT query results in memory for the lifetime of a transaction, so repeated reads in the same transaction avoid database access. Covers which entity operations are cached (read, nsread, nkread, readmulti variants, and handcrafted SQL with SELECT), plus exceptions: FOR UPDATE queries and specialized non-StandardReadMultiOperation readmulti results are not cached. Also describes invalidation on transaction commit/rollback and on insert, modify, or remove operations affecting cached tables.

children.title: How the cache gets invalidated
children.summary: The transaction SQL query cache is transaction-scoped, not global. It is invalidated when the transaction commits or rolls back, and also whenever an insert, modify, or remove updates a table tied to a cached query. Cached SELECT results are therefore only valid for the lifetime of the transaction and are cleared on data changes affecting that entry.

children.title: How to set the property for the transaction SQL cache
children.summary: Explains transaction SQL query cache behavior and configuration. Most update operations invalidate only cache entries tied to the affected table; non-SELECT ns operations invalidate the entire transaction cache. The cache is transaction-scoped only. It is enabled by default and can be disabled with curam.transaction.sqlquerycache.disabled=true in Application.prx. Large CLOB/BLOB results may cause memory issues; curam.sqlquerycache.lob.max.size limits cached LOB size, default 500KB. Also mentions the SQLQueryCacheAdmin API for runtime cache control.

children.title: SQLQueryCacheAdmin API
children.summary: This section explains transaction SQL query cache behavior and the public SQLQueryCacheAdmin API. It describes which update operations invalidate cached query results, noting that most table-based updates invalidate only related entries, while non-SELECT ns operations invalidate the whole transaction cache. It also covers cache configuration: the cache is enabled by default, can be disabled via curam.transaction.sqlquerycache.disabled, and has a LOB size limit property curam.sqlquerycache.lob.max.size (default 500KB). The API class curam.util.transaction.SQLQueryCacheAdmin exposes runtime cache control methods such as enableSQLQueryCache().

children.title: SQLQueryCacheUtil API
children.summary: Describes the transaction SQL query cache API in `curam.util.transaction.SQLQueryCacheUtil`, including `isSQLQueryCacheEnabled()` and `runWithSQLQueryCacheDisabled(Runnable)`, used when repeated reads in one transaction must bypass caching. Also covers related cache controls (`disableSQLQueryCache`, `clearSQLQueryCacheForTable`, `clearSQLQueryCache`) and ultra-verbose tracing logs for cache add, invalidate, and transaction commit/rollback invalidation events. The excerpt then begins a new topic on deferred processing for BPOs.

children.title: Logging
children.summary: Describes transaction SQL query cache logging at `Trace.kTraceUltraVerbose`. Log entries start with “Transaction SQL Query Cache:” and record when cache entries are added, invalidated, or fully cleared due to transaction commit or rollback. Also notes utility APIs for checking, disabling, and clearing the cache, including running code with caching disabled.

Explains the transaction-scoped SQL query cache in Cúram SDEJ. It caches SELECT results in memory for a transaction, speeding repeated reads, but excludes FOR UPDATE queries and specialized non-StandardReadMultiOperation readmulti results. Cached entries are invalidated on commit/rollback and when inserts, modifies, removes, or non-SELECT ns operations affect related tables. The section also covers enabling/disabling via `curam.transaction.sqlquerycache.disabled`, LOB size limits, and the `curam.util.transaction.SQLQueryCacheAdmin` runtime API.

children.title: Deferred processing
Model your deferred processes
children.summary: Explains how to model Cúram deferred processes using the <<wmdpactivity>> stereotype on a method in a <<process>> class. The method must accept ticket ID, WMInstanceData ID, and a boolean flag; these are injected by the deferred processing server after enactment, not by the caller. The section also emphasizes that deferred processes must not use Java EE transaction control (begin/commit/rollback) anywhere in the call stack. It introduces deferred process enactment via the Deferred Processing Enactment Service and the need to populate WMInstanceData with data the deferred process will use.

children.title: Deferred process enactment
WMInstanceData
children.summary: WMInstanceData defines per-instance application data for deferred processes. You create and populate this model entity with your own domain-specific properties, then pass its ID to the enactment API so the deferred method receives it as a parameter. The engine always supplies a WMInstanceData ID, but knows nothing about your business data. The section also notes offline unit testing options: set curam.test.stubdeferredprocessing to invoke deferred methods synchronously, and curam.test.stubdeferredprocessinsametransaction to run them in the same transaction.

This section explains how to enact deferred processes from a BPO using DeferredProcessing.startProcess(). It shows that you must first create and populate a WMInstanceData record with any data the deferred method will need, then pass its ID and the deferred process name (configured in DPProcess) to the enactment service. It also notes that deferred methods receive ticket ID, instance data ID, and an internal boolean flag, and that deferred processes must not use TransactionInfo or manual Java EE transaction control.

children.title: Offline Unit-Testing of Deferred Processes
children.summary: Explains how deferred processes can be unit-tested offline by invoking deferred methods synchronously when not running in an application server, using `curam.test.stubdeferredprocessing=true`. Notes that deferred invocation still depends on the caller’s transaction committing successfully, otherwise it won’t run. Also describes `curam.test.stubdeferredprocessinsametransaction=true`, which forces deferred processes to run in the same transaction; without it, they run in a separate transaction.

children.title: Configuration of Deferred Processing Table
children.summary: Explains how to configure the DPProcess table before runtime for Cúram deferred processing to work. Each deferred method modeled with the <<wmdpactivity>> stereotype must have a processName used to invoke it, along with required interfaceName and methodName, and optional ticketType and subject fields. Includes an example table population and notes that the Deferred Processing Engine provides an error-handling callback when a deferred method throws an exception.

children.title: Error Handling
children.summary: Explains how to configure the DPProcess table before runtime for Cúram deferred processing. Describes required fields for each deferred method: processName, interfaceName, and methodName, plus optional ticketType and subject. Shows example rows and notes that processName is the key used when requesting a deferred process. Begins the Error Handling subsection, stating the Deferred Processing Engine provides an error-handling callback when a deferred method fails by throwing an exception.

children.title: Security
children.summary: Deferred processing security notes: deferred processes run as user SYSTEM, so their effective locale comes from SYSTEM’s defaultLocale in the Users table; for offline unit tests, the user is blank and the server default locale is used. The section also describes error handling via the curam.util.deferredprocessing.impl.DPCallback interface and its dpHandleError() method, including global versus per-process callback configuration, and briefly summarizes deferred processing setup steps.

children.title: Deferred Processing summary
children.summary: Explains deferred processing error handling via `curam.util.deferredprocessing.impl.DPCallback` and its `dpHandleError()` method, which is invoked after a deferred message moves to the DPError queue. Covers two configuration options: a global callback via `curam.custom.deferredprocessing.dpcallback`, or a per-process callback supplied when enacting deferred processing. Notes security/locale behavior: deferred processes run as `SYSTEM`, with locale from that user; offline unit tests use the server default locale. Also summarizes implementation steps: model BPO methods with `<<wmdpactivity>>`, configure `DPProcess`, and use `DeferredProcessing`.

Deferred processing lets BPO methods run asynchronously as modeled <<wmdpactivity>> methods on <<process>> classes. The caller must create and populate a WMInstanceData record, then invoke DeferredProcessing.startProcess() with the DPProcess processName and instance data ID. Deferred methods receive ticket ID, instance data ID, and an internal boolean flag, and must not use TransactionInfo or any Java EE transaction control. The section also covers offline unit testing, DPProcess configuration, error handling via DPCallback/dpHandleError, and SYSTEM-user locale/security behavior.

children.title: Cúram timers
Java™ Platform, Enterprise Edition bean definition
children.summary: This section explains Cúram timer bean support for Java EE/EJB timers. It covers how the EJB container provides timer service for scheduling notifications on all enterprise beans except stateful session beans, with timers usable at a specific time, after a duration, or at intervals. It distinguishes single-event and interval timers, including expiration by duration or date. It also notes development support classes/interfaces, including curam.util.transaction.TimerInfo, and that outside an application server, java.util.Timer is used instead of EJB timer service.

children.title: Development support
TimerInfo Class
children.summary: This section documents the TimerInfo and TimerTask classes in curam.util.transaction/curam.util.timer. TimerInfo provides timer control APIs: startTask overloads for single-action or interval timers using duration or DateTime, plus cancel(), getID(), and isTimerTransaction(). TimerTask defines timer job metadata and parameters, including mandatory methodName, optional argument, timerName, and errorHandlerName, and read-only fields such as userID, taskID, creationTime, initialDelay, and initialEventTime.

children.title: TimerTask Class
children.summary: This section documents the timer API classes. It lists supported methods in `TimerInfo` for starting single-action and interval timers, canceling the current timer, getting the running timer ID, and checking whether the current transaction is timer-driven. It also describes `TimerTask`, which holds timer job details such as the target method, optional arguments, timer name, optional error callback, and read-only metadata like user ID, task ID, creation time, initial delay, and initial event time.

children.title: TimerCallback interface
children.summary: Defines the TimerCallback interface, used when a timed operation fails; it exposes a single handleError(Exception, TimerTask) method. Shows a sample creating a TimerTask, setting the target server operation and argument, and starting it with TimerInfo.startTask(), returning a unique timer ID. Also lists Cúram timer rules: timers can call only client-visible operations with zero or one parameter, Transactional=No, and proper access rights; timers return no value, creation/cancellation are transactional, run as the creator’s Cúram user ID, and report transaction type as online.

children.title: Code sample:
children.summary: Shows a Java code sample for starting a Cúram timer task: create TimerTask, set the server method name and optional argument struct, then call TimerInfo.startTask(10000, task) and log the returned timer ID. Also documents TimerCallback (handleError(Exception, TimerTask)) and key timer rules: only client-visible operations with zero/one parameter and Transactional=No, no return values, creation/cancellation are transactional, timers run as the creating user, and transactions report type “online.”

Defines Cúram timer development support: `TimerInfo`, `TimerTask`, and `TimerCallback`. `TimerInfo` provides APIs to start single-action or interval timers using a duration or `DateTime`, cancel the current timer, get the running timer ID, and check whether the current transaction is timer-driven. `TimerTask` holds timer metadata such as target method, optional argument, timer name, and error handler, plus read-only fields like user ID, task ID, creation time, initial delay, and first event time.

children.title: Rules for using Cúram timers
children.summary: This section lists Cúram timer usage rules and limitations. Timers can invoke only client-visible operations with zero or one parameter, Transactional set to No, and sufficient user access rights. Timers cannot return values. Timer creation and cancellation are transactional and take effect only after commit. Timer-executed transactions run under the creating user’s Cúram ID, and their transaction type reports as “online.” It also mentions the TimerCallback interface for handling timed operation failures.

children.title: Timer behavior
children.summary: Timer behavior covers when timers can be started, how they persist, and how they act after failures or restarts. Only online or timer transactions may start timers; batch/deferred processes cannot. In application servers, timers are persistent across stop/restart and resume without catching up missed ticks. Exceptions roll back the transaction but do not cancel the timer. Timers are not suitable for batch processing. The section also contrasts javax.ejb.TimerService with java.util.Timer in development, noting the latter is non-transactional and non-persistent, and explains missed ticks and initial-duration timing.

children.title: FAQ
children.summary: FAQ on Cúram timer beans and the Java EE Timer API. Explains that timers retain their own start time even if committed together, and answers common questions: how to view active timers (via TimerInfo API), how to stop timers, how to restrict methods to timer transactions, and scalability limits of large numbers of active timers. Also notes timers should be kept few and short-lived; the excerpt begins a question about timer accuracy.

Explains Cúram timer beans for scheduling client-visible operations in the future using Java EE timer service in deployed apps, or java.util.Timer in development. Covers TimerInfo APIs for starting/canceling timers and checking timer transactions, TimerTask metadata, and TimerCallback error handling. Lists key rules: only zero/one-parameter, non-transactional, authorized operations; timer actions run as the creating user; creation/cancellation are transactional; timers are persistent in app servers but not for batch work. Also details repeat/missed-tick behavior and FAQ guidance on querying, stopping, and scaling timers.

children.title: Events and event handlers
The Format of Event Files
Event definition
children.summary: Defines Cúram event files (.evx) used to declare event classes and event types for loosely coupled application communication. Explains the XML structure: root `events`, `package` for generated Java constants, optional `annotation`, `event-class` with unique runtime `value` and Java-valid `identifier`, and `event-type` entries unique within a class with their own `identifier` and runtime `value`. Includes a sample file and notes that event files are picked up from a component’s events folder and processed by build scripts.

children.title: Event handler registration
children.summary: Explains how Cúram event handlers are registered in a component’s `events/handler_config.xml`. Shows the XML structure: root `registrations`, `event-registration` entries with `handler`, optional `filter`, optional `removed="true"`, and one or more `event-class` identifiers under `event-classes`. Clarifies that handlers start when matching event classes are raised, filters must approve when present, and `removed` can disable previously registered handlers from lower-precedence components.

Explains the XML formats for Cúram event files and handler registration. Event definition files (.evx) in a component’s events folder declare event classes and event types, with root `events`, a `package` for generated constants, optional `annotation`, and unique runtime `value`/Java-valid `identifier` attributes. Event types are unique within a class. Handler registration is done in `events/handler_config.xml` under `registrations`, using `event-registration` with `handler`, optional `filter`, optional `removed`, and referenced `event-class` identifiers; handlers start when matching classes are raised and filters approve.

children.title: How to merge event files
Rules of event definition merges
children.summary: Event definition merges require custom event files to use the same filename as the existing files they extend; differing names cause load errors. Existing events cannot be removed, only extended by adding event types to an event class. Merging follows component precedence from SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, with a clean build needed after changes. This section explains rules for merging event definitions, contrasting with nearby handler merge behavior.

children.title: Rules of event handler merges
children.summary: Event handler and filter configurations are merged by handler identifier, with runtime behavior taken from the highest-precedence component in SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER. Event classes from all components are amalgamated into the merged handler configuration, and higher-precedence components can disable lower-precedence handlers using removed="true" on the event-registration element. This section also covers how event file merging works, including the need for a clean build after changing component precedence.

Explains how Cúram merges event files across components using SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER precedence. Event files can be extended by placing custom files under SERVER_DIR/components/<custom>/events without modifying core files. Event definitions must use the same filename as the file they extend; otherwise loading errors occur. Existing events cannot be removed, only extended by adding event types. Changes to component precedence require a clean build. Also distinguishes these rules from event handler/filter merging, which is covered separately.

children.title: Artifacts produced by generate events
Database Scripts
children.summary: This section explains that merged event files generate database scripts alongside Java artifacts. The scripts are placed in `/build/svr/events/gen/dmx`. It describes why event definitions are loaded into the database: administration utilities need the list of available events at runtime. It also shows example DMX output for the `EVENTCLASS` and `EVENTTYPE` tables, illustrating how event classes and event types are stored as rows in generated database scripts.

children.title: Java event code example
children.summary: Explains how Cúram generates Java constants for event classes to avoid ambiguous string literals. The generated class name matches the event class, and its fields are `EventKey` constants for each event type, with `eventClass` and `eventType` set explicitly. Also introduces how to raise an event: create and populate an `Event` struct, then call the `EventService` API so registered handlers are notified.

This section explains evgen output from merged event files: generated Java constants and DMX database scripts. Java artifacts go to /build/svr/events/gen/[package], while DMX scripts go to /build/svr/events/gen/dmx. It notes that event definitions are loaded into the database so runtime admin tools can list available events. Example DMX shows EVENTCLASS and EVENTTYPE rows, illustrating how event classes and event types are stored.

children.title: How to raise an event
children.summary: Explains how to raise a Cúram event in Java: create an event struct, populate it with data, and call the event service API. It notes that events are identified by both event class and event type, so string literals can be ambiguous; generated Java constants files help avoid incompatible values. Includes an example of a generated event-class constants file and introduces the Event and EventService classes used to raise the event.

children.title: Event handlers
children.summary: Explains how to create event handlers by implementing `curam.util.events.impl.EventHandler`. Handlers run synchronously in the same transaction as the event raiser, so exceptions roll back the transaction and long-running work should be avoided. Useful for understanding handler behavior and transaction impact when events are raised.

children.title: Event filters
children.summary: Explains event filters for Cúram event handlers: an event handler may be configured with a filter implementing `curam.util.events.impl.EventFilter`. The filter’s `accept(Event)` method decides whether a raised event is delivered to the handler; `true` invokes `eventRaised`, otherwise the event is ignored. The section then introduces Unique IDs: Cúram-generated database keys, either human-readable ascending numbers or non-human-readable random large values, and notes that key sets define reusable 2^64-ID sequences and can be configured for specific uses.

Explains Cúram events and handlers: XML event definition files (.evx) declare event classes/types, runtime values, and generated Java constants; handler_config.xml registers handlers, optional filters, and removals. Covers component merging via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER, naming rules, and that event definitions can only be extended by adding types. Describes evgen outputs (.java and .dmx), how to raise events with EventService, and how handlers run synchronously in the same transaction, so exceptions roll back and long work should be avoided.

children.title: Unique IDs
What Unique IDs are used for
children.summary: Explains Cúram-generated Unique IDs and when to use them. Unique IDs are allocated in blocks of 256 by a key server, reducing database contention by letting processes assign keys locally after one server call. This can create gaps in the sequence, and key order does not imply insertion time. The section says the key set is effectively inexhaustible and recommends Unique IDs only for database entities with no business meaning, created by multiple concurrent processes, where gaps are acceptable.

children.title: The limit of allocating Unique IDs
children.summary: This section explains Cúram-generated Unique IDs and their block-based allocation via the Key Server, which reduces database contention by avoiding long table locks. It notes that unused IDs create gaps and that key order does not reflect insertion time. It also states the practical limit: even heavy use would take over 2 million years to exhaust a key set. The section defines when Unique IDs should be used (non-business database keys under contention, with gaps acceptable) and when they should not be used.

children.title: When Unique IDs need to be used
children.summary: Use Cúram-generated Unique IDs for database entities that need a unique, non-business-meaningful key and may be created by competing online or batch processes. IDs are allocated in blocks of 256 via a key server, reducing database contention because processes use keys locally after block allocation. Gaps in the sequence are expected and acceptable, and key order does not reflect insertion order. The supply is effectively inexhaustible. Do not use them when a key needs business meaning or when sequence holes are not acceptable.

children.title: When not to use Unique IDs
children.summary: Unique IDs are Cúram-generated, block-allocated keys intended to avoid database contention and support concurrent creation of database entities. They are suitable when the key has no business meaning, multiple online/batch processes may create records, and gaps in the sequence are acceptable. This section also notes that Unique IDs can have holes, do not imply chronological insert order, and are effectively inexhaustible. Open this node to see the cases where they should not be used.

children.title: Do keys need to be human-readable?
children.summary: Explains when Unique IDs should be human-readable versus non-human-readable. Displayed IDs should generally be human-readable; otherwise non-human-readable IDs are preferred for better index distribution. If sequential, gapless human-readable IDs are required, Cúram’s key server can still leave gaps due to block allocation and rollbacks, so true no-gap sequences require application-defined key tables, at the cost of contention and performance. Also notes how to design and allocate Unique IDs using SVR_INT64 and UniqueID.nextUniqueID(), including the default non-human-readable key set.

children.title: When contiguous human-readable Unique IDs are required
children.summary: Use contiguous human-readable Unique IDs only when a business requirement demands sequential, gapless IDs (for example, Social Security numbers). Key server human-readable IDs can still have gaps because they are allocated in blocks and discarded on restart or rollback. For true no-gap sequences, Cúram uses an application-defined single-row key table updated inside the application transaction, which is slower and can become a hotspot. Use this method only when necessary and keep transactions short.

children.title: The way to design Unique IDs
children.summary: This section explains how to design Unique IDs in Cúram. It covers when to use human-readable versus non-human-readable IDs, and when contiguous, gap-free IDs are truly required. It notes that human-readable IDs from the key server can have gaps due to block allocation and rollbacks, while strict sequential IDs require application-defined key tables with higher contention and cost. It also shows how to implement IDs in UML using SVR_INT64 and generate them with UniqueID.nextUniqueID(), optionally using the default key set.

children.title: Overview of the Range Aware Key Server
Overview of the Range Aware Key Server
children.summary: Introduces the Range Aware Key Server in Cúram CTM, which generates primary keys for transported Business Object entities without clashes on target systems and can detect when transported data should update an existing entity. Explains that it uses non-overlapping key block ranges assigned per system so keys remain unique. Key ranges are configured by group number and range number, usually before first startup; if unset, the default is group 3, range 2. Once used, an allocated range cannot be removed.

children.title: How the Range Aware Key Server generates primary keys
children.summary: Explains how the Range Aware Key Server generates unique primary keys for CTM by assigning each system non-overlapping key block ranges. A system administrator configures a range using a group number and range number, usually before first startup. If none is set, the default is group 3, range 2. Keys for a system are generated only from its assigned range(s), preventing clashes with transported entities and allowing detection of existing versions for updates. The allocated range cannot be removed after first use.

children.title: Where to use the Range Aware Key Server
children.summary: Use the Range Aware Key Server only for new Key Sets created for entities that are part of transportable Business Objects. Existing Key Sets must continue using the standard Cúram SDEJ Key Server and should not be changed. The Range Aware Key Server supports both human-readable and non-human-readable generated keys, with the KeyServer table’s humanReadable attribute set to 0 or 1 as required.

Introduces the Range Aware Key Server for CTM, used to generate primary keys for transportable Business Object entities without clashes on target systems and to detect when transported data should update an existing entity. It assigns each system a non-overlapping key block range, configured by group number and range number, usually before first startup; if omitted, the default is group 3, range 2. Once a range has been used, it cannot be removed.

Explains Cúram Unique IDs for database keys: human-readable vs non-human-readable, how the key server allocates IDs in blocks of 256 to reduce contention, and why gaps and non-chronological ordering can occur. Defines when to use them: unique, non-business-meaningful keys under concurrent creation with acceptable holes. Covers when not to use them, when gapless human-readable IDs require application-defined key tables, how to generate IDs with `UniqueID.nextUniqueID()`/`SVR_INT64`, and notes key-set configuration plus Range Aware Key Server basics for CTM.

Cúram runtime behavior covers server-side transaction control, transaction-scoped SQL query caching, deferred processing, timers, events/handlers, and Unique IDs. It explains that facade methods usually run in one bean-managed transaction, with deferred processing/workflows and key-server allocation as exceptions; locking uses optimistic locking and optional forUpdate update locks. The SQL cache stores repeated SELECT results within a transaction, with invalidation rules and admin controls. It also describes async deferred methods, timer service rules, synchronous event handlers, and key-server block allocation, gaps, human-readable IDs, and Range Aware Key Server basics.

children.title: 1.5 Cúram configuration parameters
Bootstrap.properties
children.summary: Bootstrap.properties documents configuration properties for Cúram’s Bootstrap.properties file, grouped by category. This excerpt covers Database and Environment settings. Database properties include DB type, username/password, Oracle prepared-statement and connection cache controls, z/OS tablespace and foreign-key behavior, and a switch to disable foreign-key generation. Environment properties cover application server vendor, tnamseserv port, bindings location, default date/time formats and separators, dynamic property access, deprecation reporting, entity struct deprecation, and money rounding behavior.

children.title: Dynamic properties in Application.prx
Dynamic properties in Application.prx — pages 174-183
children.summary: This section lists dynamic Application.prx properties grouped by area: Environment, JMX, Test, Rules, IEG, Custom, Trace, and Security. It documents property names, types, defaults, and effects, including transaction SQL query caching, audit data compression, JMX monitoring/tracing/statistics, test-only overrides and stubs, rules engine behavior, IEG answer-validation behavior, custom callback class hooks, and trace levels and diagnostics controls. It is mainly a reference for configuring server/runtime behavior and troubleshooting.

children.title: Dynamic properties in Application.prx — pages 184-187
children.summary: This section lists dynamic Application.prx properties for configuring Cúram security, SMTP mail, XML Server, database behavior, KeyServer, and BatchLauncher. It includes defaults and meanings for key settings such as login/break-in thresholds, password warning periods, mail server host/port/timeouts, XML server host/port/printer/encoding, database readmulti limits and lock timeout, key server retry and remaining-key notifications, and batch error email, default error code, and DB-to-JMS notification/SSL options. Useful if you need runtime environment tuning or property names.

Reference list of dynamic Application.prx properties from test/custom through environment, JMX, rules, IEG, security, SMTP, XML Server, database, KeyServer, and BatchLauncher. It documents names, types, defaults, and effects for runtime tuning, tracing, auditing, caching, test overrides, rule/IEG behavior, authentication thresholds, mail/XML server setup, DB readmulti/lock/batch limits, key allocation/retry alerts, and batch error/D2JMS notification options. Useful when you need a property name or to adjust server/runtime behavior.

children.title: Static properties in Application.prx
KeyServer
children.summary: Explains the KeyServer mechanism for generating Cúram Unique IDs and how to tune batch performance with `curam.keyserver.keyset.cachesize`. The setting controls how many 256-ID key sets are cached per KeyServer transaction (default 1, range 1–64). Examples show configuring it in `Bootstrap.properties` or via `runbatch` command line for all batch programs or a single batch job. Notes that it affects batch programs only, not online, workflow, deferred processing, or range-aware key sets.

Static properties in Application.prx covers built-in configuration groups for Workflow, CTM, Custom handlers, Security, Trace, Environment, and KeyServer. It lists notable properties such as workflow audit suppression flags, custom audit/authentication hook classes, security case-sensitivity and authorization controls, trace handler and SQL string length limits, project/tab cache settings, and KeyServer tuning. The KeyServer section explains `curam.keyserver.keyset.cachesize` (default 1, range 1–64) for batch-only unique ID caching, with Bootstrap.properties and `runbatch` examples, and notes it does not affect online, workflow, deferred, or range-aware key sets.

children.title: Variable property settings
children.summary: Defines variable property settings for runtime configuration. Covers transaction properties, including per-operation transaction timeouts using a fully qualified class/method naming pattern, plus a LoginBeanTransaction timeout that defaults to the application server JTA timeout if unspecified. Also covers auditing properties: operation-level auditing (curam.audit.opaudittrail) and table-level auditing (curam.audit.audittrail), both boolean flags enabled/disabled per project/code package/class and defaulting to model settings.

Cúram configuration parameters explains where runtime settings are defined in .properties and .prx files, with BOOLEAN values treating true/yes case-insensitively. It covers Bootstrap.properties database and environment settings (DB type, credentials, Oracle cache/connection cache, z/OS tablespace/foreign-key controls, application server, date/time formats, deprecation, dynamic-property access, money rounding), plus test and custom hooks. It then documents Application.prx dynamic/static properties for environment, transaction/cache, audit, security, trace, workflow, CTM, KeyServer batch key-set caching, and variable transaction/audit settings.

children.title: 1.6 Infrastructure auditing settings
Default table-level audit setting
Default table-level audit setting — pages 194-203
children.summary: Lists Cúram infrastructure auditing settings and the default table-level audit flag for database operations. It explains that these defaults can be overridden by application properties, and that some operations are N/A because they do not support auditing (e.g., handcrafted SQL). The pages then enumerate many operation names with their default auditing status (True, False, or N/A), covering areas like ActivityInstance, AppResource, AuthenticationLog, batch objects, code tables, event/IEG objects, security, JMS, keys, and process data.

children.title: Default table-level audit setting — pages 204-213
children.summary: Lists default auditing settings for many Cúram server database/table operations. Most operations are set to False, some are N/A, and a few security- and properties-related inserts/modifies are True. Covered areas include ProcessDefinition/Instance, PropDescription/Properties, RuleSet and Script entities, SecurityGroup/Role/Identifier links, Task and TaskHistory, TransitionInstance, Users, WDO/template/history, WorkflowDeadline, and WorkflowHistory. Useful as a reference table for checking which CRUD/search operations are audited by default.

children.title: Default table-level audit setting — pages 214-216
children.summary: Lists default table-level auditing settings for Cúram 8.1.2, showing many operations with auditing disabled (False), including WorkflowHistory, XMLArchiveDoc, XSLTemplate, and XSLTemplateInst methods. The remainder of the section is legal and licensing material: permissions for personal/commercial use, restrictions on redistribution and derivative works, export compliance, warranty disclaimers, sample code license terms, privacy policy link, and trademark notices.

Reference tables for Cúram infrastructure auditing settings. Explains audit-related properties and that table-level audit defaults can be overridden; some operations are N/A when auditing isn’t supported. Then enumerates many database operation names with default auditing flags (True/False/N/A) across activity, app resource, authentication, batch, code tables, events, security, JMS, task/workflow, users, WDO, XML/XSL, etc. The last pages are legal notices, license terms, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Lists Cúram infrastructure auditing settings and the default table-level auditing flags for many database operations. Explains that defaults can be overridden by application properties, and that some handcrafted SQL operations are not auditable and are marked N/A. Covers operation groups across activity, app resource, authentication/authorisation, batch, code tables, events, security, JMS, tasks/workflow, users, WDO, XML/XSL, etc. The section ends with legal notices, licensing, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Introduces the Cúram server development environment for model-driven client/server generation. Covers application/component directory structure, component precedence via SERVER_COMPONENT_ORDER and locale-based overrides, and build artifacts/targets. Explains configuration and runtime settings, plus Data Manager for database creation/loading and SQL Checker for handcrafted SQL validation. Also includes SDEJ setup and API usage, application resources and locale-aware storage, runtime behavior (transactions, caching, deferred processing, timers, IDs), configuration parameters, and infrastructure auditing defaults.
